On the CSP exam, understand that post-incident response must address physical safety, psychological well-being, investigation and documentation, and prevention of future incidents.

diagrams.title: Four Types of Workplace Violence
rows: Security, access control, lighting, cash handling

diagrams.title: Workplace Violence Prevention Program
steps: Management Commitment - Zero-tolerance policy, resource allocation
Hazard Assessment - Analyze risk factors by workplace type
Prevention Controls - Security measures, environmental design, staffing
Training - De-escalation, threat recognition, response procedures
Incident Response - Emergency procedures, law enforcement coordination
Post-Incident Support - EAP, debriefing, lessons learned, policy review

mnemonics.title: Workplace Violence Classification
mnemonics.description: I = Criminal Intent (no relationship with workplace, robbery/theft), II = Customer/Client (service recipient, most common in healthcare), III = Worker-on-Worker (current/former employee), IV = Personal Relationship (domestic violence spillover). Memory aid: "Criminals, Customers, Coworkers, Companions" - the four C's in order.

examTips: Know the four types of workplace violence: Type I (criminal intent, no relationship), Type II (customer/client, most common in healthcare), Type III (worker-on-worker), Type IV (personal relationship/domestic spillover).
Type I accounts for 75-80% of workplace HOMICIDES. Type II is the most common type overall when including non-fatal assaults, especially in healthcare.
OSHA has NO specific workplace violence standard. Enforcement is through the General Duty Clause, Section 5(a)(1) of the OSH Act.
Prevention program elements: management commitment, worksite analysis, hazard prevention and control, training, recordkeeping/evaluation, and a zero-tolerance policy.
De-escalation techniques: calm voice, non-threatening body language, active listening, acknowledge feelings, maintain safe distance (2 arm lengths), ensure escape route.
A behavioral threat assessment team should be multidisciplinary (HR, security, legal, EAP, management) and evaluate threats using a structured process.
Risk factors for workplace violence: working with public, handling money/valuables, working alone, late-night hours, delivering services, working in high-crime areas.
Post-incident response must address: medical treatment, scene security, law enforcement, documentation, psychological support (voluntary CISD/CISM), investigation, and prevention improvements.
regulations: OSHA General Duty Clause - Section 5(a)(1) of the OSH Act
OSHA Workplace Violence Prevention Guidelines for Healthcare and Social Services
OSHA Recommendations for Workplace Violence Prevention in Late-Night Retail

topicContent.overview:
Environmental protection and pollution prevention programs focus on reducing or eliminating waste and pollutants at the source rather than treating them after generation. The Pollution Prevention Act of 1990 established a national policy that pollution should be prevented or reduced at the source whenever feasible, and that pollution that cannot be prevented should be recycled, treated, or disposed of in an environmentally safe manner as a last resort. For the CSP exam, this domain represents approximately 6% of all questions, and candidates must understand the pollution prevention hierarchy, SPCC requirements, secondary containment calculations, abatement technologies, and stormwater management under the NPDES permit program.

In real-world practice, a safety professional at a manufacturing facility might implement a pollution prevention program by first conducting a waste audit to identify all waste streams, then systematically applying the pollution prevention hierarchy to each stream. For example, a metal finishing operation generating chromium-bearing wastewater might switch from hexavalent chromium plating to trivalent chromium (source reduction), recover and reuse rinse water through counter-current rinsing (recycling), treat remaining wastewater through chemical precipitation (treatment), and only then dispose of treatment sludge in a permitted facility (disposal). This systematic approach not only reduces regulatory burden and liability but often saves money through reduced raw material purchases, lower waste disposal costs, and decreased regulatory compliance expenses.

The regulatory framework for pollution prevention spans multiple federal statutes including the Clean Water Act, Clean Air Act, RCRA, and the Pollution Prevention Act itself. State and local regulations often impose additional requirements. Safety professionals must navigate these overlapping regulations while developing practical, cost-effective solutions that protect both workers and the environment. Understanding spill prevention, secondary containment design, air and water treatment technologies, and stormwater management are essential competencies tested on the CSP examination.

concepts.title: Source Reduction
concepts.description: Source reduction is the elimination or reduction of pollution at its origin and represents the most preferred approach in the pollution prevention hierarchy established by the Pollution Prevention Act of 1990. Methods include process changes such as switching from solvent-based to water-based coatings, material substitution such as replacing hazardous chemicals with less toxic alternatives, equipment modifications such as installing closed-loop cooling systems to eliminate water discharge, and product reformulation such as designing products that generate less waste during manufacturing. For example, a furniture manufacturer that switches from solvent-based lacquers to UV-cured water-based finishes can eliminate over 90% of VOC emissions while often achieving superior product quality. Source reduction is preferred because it avoids the costs and risks associated with managing pollution after it is generated, including treatment costs, disposal liability, regulatory compliance burden, and potential cleanup costs if releases occur. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand that source reduction always ranks first in the pollution prevention hierarchy and should be able to identify source reduction opportunities in workplace scenarios. The EPA estimates that source reduction can save companies 3-5 times the cost of end-of-pipe treatment for equivalent pollution reduction.

concepts.title: Spill Prevention Control & Countermeasure (SPCC)
concepts.description: The SPCC rule, codified at 40 CFR 112, requires facilities that store oil above specific threshold quantities to prepare and implement spill prevention plans to protect navigable waters and adjoining shorelines from oil spills. The threshold triggers are 1,320 gallons aggregate aboveground oil storage capacity or 42,000 gallons total underground oil storage capacity, and only containers of 55 gallons or larger are counted toward the aggregate aboveground threshold. The SPCC plan must be prepared by a Professional Engineer (PE) who certifies the plan, and it must be reviewed and amended every five years or whenever there is a change in facility design, construction, operation, or maintenance that affects the potential for an oil discharge. Key plan elements include facility diagrams, oil inventory and storage information, discharge prevention measures, discharge or drainage controls, countermeasures for discharge discovery and response, methods of disposal of recovered materials, and contact information for response personnel. For example, a distribution warehouse with ten 300-gallon aboveground heating oil tanks totaling 3,000 gallons would trigger SPCC requirements and need secondary containment, regular inspections, employee training, and a PE-certified plan. On the CSP exam, knowing the exact threshold quantities (1,320 gal aboveground aggregate and 42,000 gal underground) is critical, as is understanding that the plan must be PE-certified and reviewed at least every five years.

concepts.title: Secondary Containment
concepts.description: Secondary containment provides backup containment to capture spills or leaks from primary storage containers and is a fundamental requirement of SPCC plans and many state environmental regulations. The sizing requirement for secondary containment is that it must hold the greater of either 110% of the volume of the largest single container within the containment area or 10% of the total aggregate volume of all containers in the containment area. For example, if a containment area holds one 10,000-gallon tank and four 2,500-gallon tanks (total aggregate 20,000 gallons), the secondary containment must hold the greater of 11,000 gallons (110% of the largest container) or 2,000 gallons (10% of aggregate), so the containment must be at least 11,000 gallons. Common types of secondary containment include concrete dikes and berms around tank farms, containment pallets and sumps for drum storage areas, double-walled tanks with interstitial monitoring, and vault systems for underground tanks. Rainfall must be considered in outdoor containment design because accumulated rainwater reduces available containment capacity, so facilities must either size the containment to account for a 25-year, 24-hour rainfall event or provide adequate drainage controls with valves that remain closed except during supervised drainage of uncontaminated stormwater. The CSP exam frequently tests the 110%/10% rule and requires candidates to calculate which value governs in a given scenario.

concepts.title: Abatement Techniques
concepts.description: Abatement techniques are engineering methods used to reduce pollution after it has been generated, representing the treatment level of the pollution prevention hierarchy. Air pollution abatement technologies include wet scrubbers that use liquid sprays to capture particulates and soluble gases with efficiencies of 90-99%, dry scrubbers that inject dry reagent or slurry to neutralize acid gases, electrostatic precipitators (ESPs) that use high-voltage electric fields to charge and collect particulates with efficiencies exceeding 99% for large installations, baghouse filters that capture particulates on fabric filter bags achieving 99%+ collection efficiency for fine particulates, catalytic converters that chemically transform pollutants such as converting CO and hydrocarbons to CO2 and water, and activated carbon adsorption systems that capture VOCs and other organic compounds on porous carbon media. Water pollution abatement includes clarification using settling tanks and chemical flocculation to remove suspended solids, filtration through sand, multimedia, or membrane filters, chemical treatment such as neutralization of acids/bases and oxidation/reduction reactions, biological treatment using activated sludge or trickling filter systems to decompose organic pollutants, and reverse osmosis for removing dissolved contaminants. Soil remediation techniques include excavation and off-site disposal at permitted facilities, soil vapor extraction (SVE) for volatile organic compounds, bioremediation using naturally occurring or enhanced microbial degradation, and in-situ chemical oxidation (ISCO) to destroy contaminants in place. For the CSP exam, candidates should know the general application and relative effectiveness of each technology and be able to match the appropriate abatement technique to a given pollutant type.

concepts.title: Best Management Practices (BMPs)
concepts.description: Best Management Practices are operational procedures, structural controls, and management activities that prevent or reduce the discharge of pollutants to the environment, serving as the practical implementation of pollution prevention principles in daily operations. Operational BMPs include good housekeeping practices such as regular cleaning of work areas and proper storage of materials, preventive maintenance programs that inspect and repair equipment before leaks develop, proper materials handling procedures including safe transfer techniques and drip pan use during fluid transfers, employee training on pollution prevention and spill response, and record-keeping of inspections, maintenance, and incidents. Structural BMPs include covered storage areas to keep precipitation from contacting stored materials, curbing and grading to direct runoff away from pollutant sources, oil/water separators at vehicle maintenance areas, sediment and erosion controls such as silt fences and sediment basins during construction, and vegetated buffer strips and bioswales to filter stormwater runoff. For example, a concrete batch plant might implement BMPs including covered aggregate storage to prevent stormwater contact, wash water recycling systems, dust suppression on haul roads, concrete washout containment areas, and regular pH monitoring of site runoff. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand that BMPs are preventive measures that form the foundation of any pollution prevention program and that they are often required as conditions of NPDES stormwater permits. Effective BMP programs typically reduce both environmental risk and operational costs by preventing spills and waste generation before they occur.

concepts.title: Stormwater Management
concepts.description: Stormwater management involves preventing pollutants from being carried by rainwater runoff into receiving waters, and it is regulated primarily through the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit program under the Clean Water Act. Industrial facilities that discharge stormwater must obtain NPDES permits, which typically require development of a Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP), implementation of BMPs, regular visual monitoring and in some cases analytical monitoring of stormwater discharges, and benchmark monitoring for specific pollutants based on the industrial sector. The Multi-Sector General Permit (MSGP) covers 29 industrial sectors with sector-specific requirements, while construction activities disturbing one acre or more require a Construction General Permit (CGP) with erosion and sediment controls. For example, an auto salvage yard under the MSGP must implement BMPs for fluid management, parts cleaning, battery storage, and vehicle storage areas, and must conduct quarterly visual monitoring of stormwater discharges plus annual analytical monitoring for Total Suspended Solids, Chemical Oxygen Demand, and other sector-specific parameters. Key stormwater BMPs include covered storage of materials and waste containers, good housekeeping and regular sweeping of paved areas, oil/water separators at vehicle maintenance areas, detention basins and constructed wetlands for flow control and treatment, and regular inspection and maintenance of all stormwater controls. For the CSP exam, candidates should understand that NPDES permits are required for industrial stormwater discharges, that SWPPPs are a central element of compliance, and that the permit program is typically administered by authorized state agencies rather than directly by EPA in most states.

concepts.title: Air Pollution Details - PM and VOCs
concepts.description: Particulate matter is classified by aerodynamic diameter: PM10 (particles 10 micrometers or smaller, penetrate upper airways) and PM2.5 (particles 2.5 micrometers or smaller, penetrate deep into lungs and enter bloodstream - more dangerous). Sources include combustion, grinding, crushing, and vehicle emissions. Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) are carbon-containing chemicals that easily evaporate at room temperature. Sources: paints, solvents, adhesives, fuels, and industrial processes. VOCs react with nitrogen oxides (NOx) in sunlight to form ground-level ozone (smog), which causes respiratory problems. Acid rain forms when SO2 and NOx emissions react with water vapor in the atmosphere to produce sulfuric and nitric acids, damaging ecosystems, buildings, and water bodies. The Clean Air Act establishes National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for six criteria pollutants: CO, Pb, NO2, O3, PM (PM2.5 and PM10), and SO2. HAPs (Hazardous Air Pollutants) are a separate category of 187 toxic air pollutants regulated under Section 112 of the CAA. Example: A manufacturing plant emits 15 tons/year of toluene (a VOC and HAP). This exceeds the major source threshold of 10 tons/year for any single HAP, requiring a Title V operating permit and compliance with Maximum Achievable Control Technology (MACT) standards.

concepts.title: Wastewater Treatment Fundamentals
concepts.description: Wastewater treatment is a critical environmental management process that removes contaminants from industrial and municipal wastewater before discharge to receiving waters. Understanding treatment principles is important for safety professionals managing environmental compliance. Treatment occurs in stages: (1) Preliminary Treatment - screening and grit removal to protect downstream equipment from damage by large solids, rags, and abrasive materials. (2) Primary Treatment - physical settling (sedimentation) in large clarifiers where suspended solids settle by gravity, removing approximately 50-70% of suspended solids and 25-40% of BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand). BOD measures the amount of oxygen consumed by microorganisms in decomposing organic matter - higher BOD indicates more pollution. (3) Secondary Treatment - biological processes where microorganisms consume dissolved organic matter. The activated sludge process is most common: wastewater is mixed with microorganisms in aeration tanks where air is supplied to support biological activity, then separated in secondary clarifiers. This removes 85-95% of BOD and suspended solids. Other biological methods include trickling filters, rotating biological contactors, and constructed wetlands. (4) Tertiary/Advanced Treatment - additional processes to remove nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus) that cause eutrophication, and further polishing through filtration, disinfection (chlorination, UV, ozone), and sometimes activated carbon adsorption. Key parameters monitored: BOD (biological oxygen demand - target typically <30 mg/L for discharge), COD (chemical oxygen demand - includes non-biodegradable organics), TSS (total suspended solids - target typically <30 mg/L), pH (6-9 for most permits), and fecal coliform bacteria. Safety hazards in wastewater treatment: hydrogen sulfide (H2S) in enclosed spaces (olfactory fatigue at >100 ppm - workers cannot smell it), methane from anaerobic digestion (explosion risk), biological hazards from pathogens, drowning hazards, chemical handling (chlorine gas, sodium hypochlorite), and confined space entry for tank maintenance. NPDES (National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System) permits under the Clean Water Act regulate discharge limits for specific parameters. Example: A food processing plant discharges wastewater with BOD of 800 mg/L (high organic load from food waste). The facility installs an on-site treatment system: screening and equalization, followed by an activated sludge system with extended aeration, secondary clarification, and UV disinfection. The treated effluent achieves BOD <20 mg/L and TSS <15 mg/L, meeting their NPDES permit limits for discharge to the local river. Reference: Fundamentals of Wastewater Treatment and Engineering (Riffat), Accident Prevention Manual - Environmental Management (NSC).

diagrams.title: Wastewater Treatment Process Stages
steps: Secondary: Biological Treatment - Activated Sludge (85-95% BOD removal)
Discharge: Meet NPDES permit limits (BOD <30, TSS <30 mg/L)

cards.title: Clean Air Act (CAA)
cards.description: NAAQS for 6 criteria pollutants, NSR permits, Title V operating permits, MACT standards for HAPs

cards.title: Clean Water Act (CWA)
cards.description: NPDES discharge permits, effluent limits, wetlands protection (Section 404), SPCC plans

cards.title: RCRA
cards.description: Cradle-to-grave hazardous waste management. Generator categories: LQG, SQG, VSQG. Manifest system.

cards.title: CERCLA (Superfund)
cards.description: Cleanup of contaminated sites. Strict, joint & several liability. RQ reporting. PRPs pay for cleanup.

cards.title: EPCRA / SARA Title III
cards.description: Community right-to-know. TRI reporting (Form R). Emergency planning. Tier I/II chemical inventories.

cards.title: NEPA
cards.description: Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) for federal actions. FONSI if no significant impact. Public comment process.

examTips: Pollution prevention hierarchy: Source Reduction (most preferred) > Recycling > Treatment > Disposal (least preferred). The 1990 Pollution Prevention Act established this as national policy.
SPCC plan required for oil storage: 1,320 gal aggregate aboveground OR 42,000 gal underground. Only containers 55 gallons or larger count. Plan must be PE-certified and reviewed every 5 years.
Secondary containment: 110% of largest container OR 10% of aggregate, whichever is GREATER. For outdoor containment, also account for 25-year/24-hour rainfall event.
Know the major air pollution control technologies: wet/dry scrubbers for gases, ESPs and baghouse filters for particulates, activated carbon for VOCs, catalytic converters for combustion products.
NPDES permits are required for industrial stormwater discharges. A SWPPP (Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan) is a key compliance document.

topicContent.overview:
Hazardous materials management involves the proper classification, storage, handling, and security of materials that pose risks to health, safety, or the environment. The Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS) was adopted by OSHA through the 2012 revision of the Hazard Communication Standard (29 CFR 1910.1200) and provides a standardized approach to chemical hazard classification and communication worldwide. GHS replaced the previous system of Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS) with the standardized 16-section Safety Data Sheet (SDS) format and introduced nine standardized pictograms, two signal words, and harmonized hazard and precautionary statements. For the CSP exam, candidates must understand GHS classification categories, SDS sections, chemical storage segregation rules, and RCRA hazardous waste generator requirements.

In practice, a safety professional managing hazardous materials at a chemical distribution warehouse would need to ensure that all incoming chemicals are properly classified under GHS, that SDSs are maintained and readily accessible for every hazardous chemical on-site, that chemicals are stored according to compatibility requirements with proper segregation of incompatible materials, and that employees are trained on the specific hazards of chemicals in their work areas. For example, storing nitric acid (an oxidizing acid) in the same cabinet as acetone (a flammable solvent) could lead to a violent fire or explosion, so these must be segregated in dedicated storage areas with appropriate ventilation, containment, and fire protection. Chemical inventory management, proper labeling during transfer and use, exposure monitoring, and emergency response planning all fall within the scope of hazardous materials management.

The Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) regulates hazardous waste from the point of generation through transportation, treatment, storage, and disposal, creating a comprehensive cradle-to-grave management system. Generators are classified based on monthly waste generation quantities into Large Quantity Generators (LQG), Small Quantity Generators (SQG), and Very Small Quantity Generators (VSQG), each with different requirements for accumulation time limits, training, emergency planning, and reporting. Understanding these generator categories, their thresholds, and their specific regulatory obligations is essential knowledge for the CSP examination.

concepts.title: GHS Classification
concepts.description: The Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS) classifies chemical hazards into two main groups: health hazards and physical hazards, with environmental hazards as an additional category. Health hazard classes include acute toxicity (Categories 1-5, with 1 being most severe), skin corrosion/irritation, serious eye damage/irritation, respiratory or skin sensitization, germ cell mutagenicity, carcinogenicity, reproductive toxicity, specific target organ toxicity (single and repeated exposure), and aspiration hazard. Physical hazard classes include flammable gases/liquids/solids/aerosols, oxidizing gases/liquids/solids, gases under pressure, self-reactive substances, pyrophoric liquids/solids, self-heating substances, substances that emit flammable gas in contact with water, organic peroxides, corrosive to metals, and explosives. Each hazard class is divided into categories based on severity, with Category 1 always being the most severe. GHS uses standardized label elements including pictograms, signal words (Danger for more severe hazards, Warning for less severe), hazard statements (H-codes describing the nature of the hazard), and precautionary statements (P-codes describing protective measures). For example, a chemical classified as Acute Toxicity Category 1 (Oral) would carry the skull and crossbones pictogram, the signal word Danger, and the hazard statement H300 (Fatal if swallowed). On the CSP exam, knowing that Danger is always more severe than Warning and understanding the general classification framework is essential.

concepts.title: GHS Pictograms
concepts.description: GHS uses nine standardized pictograms, each displayed as a red-bordered diamond (rhombus) with a black symbol on a white background, to visually communicate specific categories of chemical hazards. The Flame pictogram indicates flammable gases, liquids, solids, and aerosols as well as pyrophoric, self-heating, and self-reactive substances and those that emit flammable gas when in contact with water. The Flame Over Circle indicates oxidizers (gases, liquids, and solids) that can intensify fires. The Exploding Bomb represents explosives, self-reactive substances, and organic peroxides that may explode. The Skull and Crossbones signals acute toxicity hazards in Categories 1-3 (fatal or toxic by various routes of exposure). The Corrosion pictogram indicates substances causing skin corrosion, serious eye damage, or corrosion to metals. The Exclamation Mark covers less severe acute toxicity (Category 4), skin and eye irritation, skin sensitization, specific target organ toxicity (single exposure Category 3), and respiratory tract irritation. The Health Hazard (silhouette of a person with a starburst on chest) indicates carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, reproductive toxicity, respiratory sensitization, specific target organ toxicity, and aspiration hazard. The Environment pictogram shows a dead fish and tree and indicates aquatic toxicity. The Gas Cylinder indicates gases under pressure, including compressed, liquefied, refrigerated liquefied, and dissolved gases. For the CSP exam, candidates must know all nine pictograms and the types of hazards each represents, and should be able to identify which pictogram applies to a given hazard scenario.

concepts.title: Safety Data Sheets (SDS)
concepts.description: The Safety Data Sheet is a standardized 16-section document required by GHS/HazCom 2012 that provides comprehensive information about a hazardous chemical. Section 1 covers Identification including product name, manufacturer, emergency phone number, and recommended use. Section 2 (Hazard Identification) is one of the most critical sections, containing GHS classification, pictograms, signal word, hazard and precautionary statements, and any hazards not otherwise classified. Section 3 covers Composition/Information on Ingredients. Section 4 addresses First-Aid Measures. Section 5 covers Fire-Fighting Measures including suitable extinguishing media. Section 6 addresses Accidental Release Measures for spill response. Section 7 covers Handling and Storage including incompatibilities. Section 8 (Exposure Controls/Personal Protection) is another critical section containing occupational exposure limits such as PELs, TLVs, and RELs, along with specific PPE recommendations and engineering controls. Section 9 lists Physical and Chemical Properties including flash point, boiling point, vapor pressure, and other key data. Section 10 covers Stability and Reactivity including conditions to avoid. Section 11 addresses Toxicological Information. Section 12 covers Ecological Information. Section 13 addresses Disposal Considerations. Section 14 (Transport Information) provides DOT, IATA, and IMDG classification data for shipping. Section 15 covers Regulatory Information. Section 16 addresses Other Information including date of preparation or last revision. For the CSP exam, candidates should know that the SDS has exactly 16 sections in a fixed order and should be especially familiar with Sections 2 (Hazards), 8 (Exposure Controls/PPE), and 14 (Transport) as these are most frequently referenced by safety professionals and tested on the exam.

concepts.title: Chemical Storage
concepts.description: Chemical storage segregation is a critical safety practice that prevents dangerous reactions between incompatible chemicals during storage, and it is governed by principles of chemical compatibility as well as regulations from OSHA, NFPA, and EPA. Fundamental segregation rules require that acids be separated from bases because mixing can cause violent exothermic reactions, oxidizers must be segregated from flammables and organic materials because oxidizers can greatly intensify fires, water-reactive chemicals (such as sodium, potassium, lithium, and calcium carbide) must be stored in dry locations completely isolated from water sources, and poisons/toxics should be separated from food, food-related items, and incompatible chemicals. NFPA 400 (Hazardous Materials Code) and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106 provide specific requirements for flammable and combustible liquid storage including maximum container sizes, storage cabinet requirements (no more than 60 gallons of Class I and Class II flammable liquids per cabinet, maximum three cabinets per fire area without additional protection), and storage room requirements based on quantities. For example, in a laboratory setting, concentrated nitric acid (an oxidizing acid) must be stored separately from acetic acid (an organic acid) because nitric acid can react violently with organic materials, and both must be separated from sodium hydroxide (a base). Storage areas must have adequate ventilation to prevent accumulation of vapors, secondary containment to capture leaks, proper labeling on all containers, temperature controls where required, and restricted access to prevent unauthorized use. On the CSP exam, candidates should be able to identify incompatible chemical combinations and understand basic storage quantity limits for flammable liquids in cabinets and storage rooms.

concepts.title: Hazardous Waste Generator Categories
concepts.description: The Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) classifies hazardous waste generators into three categories based on the amount of hazardous waste generated per calendar month, each with different regulatory requirements for accumulation, storage, training, and reporting. Large Quantity Generators (LQG) generate 1,000 kilograms (2,200 pounds) or more of hazardous waste per month, or more than 1 kilogram of acutely hazardous waste per month. LQGs may accumulate hazardous waste on-site for up to 90 days without a storage permit, must have a formal written contingency plan and emergency procedures, must conduct annual training for all employees handling hazardous waste, must submit biennial reports to EPA, and must maintain detailed records. Small Quantity Generators (SQG) generate between 100 and 1,000 kilograms (220-2,200 pounds) of hazardous waste per month. SQGs may accumulate waste on-site for up to 270 days (or 360 days if waste must be shipped more than 200 miles), are limited to accumulating no more than 6,000 kilograms on-site at any time, and must have a basic emergency plan and ensure employees are familiar with waste handling procedures. Very Small Quantity Generators (VSQG, formerly Conditionally Exempt Small Quantity Generators) generate 100 kilograms or less of hazardous waste per month and no more than 1 kilogram of acutely hazardous waste per month. VSQGs have no specific accumulation time limit but may not accumulate more than 1,000 kilograms on-site at any time, and must identify their hazardous waste and send it to a permitted facility. For the CSP exam, memorize the generation thresholds (1,000 kg for LQG, 100 kg for SQG), the storage time limits (90 days for LQG, 270 days for SQG), and the key differences in requirements between categories. A common exam scenario involves determining a facility generator category based on monthly waste generation quantities.

diagrams.title: GHS Hazard Pictograms (9 Symbols)
rows: Skin corrosion, eye damage, metal corrosion

steps.title: Section 1: Identification
steps.description: Product name, manufacturer, emergency phone, recommended use

steps.title: Section 2: Hazard Identification
steps.description: GHS classification, pictograms, signal word, H-statements, P-statements

steps.title: Section 8: Exposure Controls/PPE
steps.description: OELs (PEL, TLV, REL), engineering controls, PPE recommendations

steps.title: Section 9: Physical/Chemical Properties
steps.description: Flash point, boiling point, vapor pressure, specific gravity, pH

steps.title: Section 14: Transport Information
steps.description: DOT, IATA, IMDG shipping classification, UN number, packing group

cards.title: Flammables
cards.description: Store in approved cabinets (max 60 gal Class I/II). Separate from oxidizers. Ground and bond when dispensing.

cards.title: Acids
cards.description: Separate from bases, organics, metals. Corrosion-resistant containment. Fume hood for volatile acids.

cards.title: Oxidizers
cards.description: Isolate from flammables, organics, reducing agents. Can intensify fires dramatically.

cards.title: Water-Reactive
cards.description: Store in dry locations. Isolate from water sources, sprinklers. Na, K, Li, CaC2.

cards.title: Toxics/Poisons
cards.description: Separate from food items. Locked storage for acute toxins. Ventilated cabinet for volatile toxics.

mnemonics.title: Key SDS Sections
mnemonics.description: Section 2 (Hazards) and Section 8 (Exposure Controls/PPE) are most commonly referenced by safety professionals. Section 14 = Transport. Section 15 = Regulatory. Remember: all 16 sections must appear in the specified order.

mnemonics.title: Danger vs. Warning
mnemonics.description: Danger = more severe hazard. Warning = less severe hazard. A chemical can only have ONE signal word. If multiple hazards apply, the most severe signal word is used. If Danger applies, Warning is never also shown.

mnemonics.title: RCRA Generator Categories
mnemonics.description: LQG: 1,000+ kg/month, 90-day storage. SQG: 100-1,000 kg/month, 270-day storage. VSQG: <100 kg/month, no time limit but 1,000 kg max on-site. Remember: Larger generators = shorter storage time (more waste = more risk = stricter rules).

examTips: GHS has 9 pictograms displayed as red-bordered diamonds. "Danger" is always more severe than "Warning" as signal words. A chemical can have only ONE signal word.
SDS must have exactly 16 sections in the specified order. Know key sections: 2 (Hazards), 8 (Exposure/PPE), 14 (Transport). SDSs replaced the old MSDS format under HazCom 2012.

RCRA hazardous waste characteristics: Ignitability, Corrosivity, Reactivity, Toxicity (I-C-R-T). Waste codes: D-001 (Ignitability), D-002 (Corrosivity), D-003 (Reactivity), D-004+ (Toxicity via TCLP).
LQG can store hazardous waste up to 90 days without a permit (generates 1,000+ kg/month). SQG up to 270 days (100-1,000 kg/month). VSQG has no time limit but max 1,000 kg on-site (<100 kg/month).
Chemical storage: Acids separated from bases, oxidizers from flammables, water-reactive kept dry. NFPA allows max 60 gallons of Class I/II flammable liquids per storage cabinet.

The Health Hazard pictogram (silhouette with starburst on chest) indicates chronic hazards: carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, reproductive toxicity, respiratory sensitization, and specific target organ toxicity.

topicContent.overview:
Waste management involves the comprehensive cradle-to-grave management of waste materials from generation through collection, transport, treatment, and final disposal or recycling. For safety professionals, waste management encompasses understanding hazardous waste characteristics and classification under RCRA, the universal waste program, proper labeling and manifesting requirements, disposal technologies, site remediation under CERCLA (Superfund), and the interplay between waste management regulations and occupational safety requirements such as HAZWOPER (29 CFR 1910.120). The CSP exam tests candidates on RCRA hazardous waste characteristics (ICRT), waste codes, generator categories, manifest requirements, disposal methods, universal waste rules, and CERCLA liability provisions.

In practice, a safety professional at a chemical manufacturing plant might manage dozens of distinct waste streams, each requiring proper characterization to determine if the waste is hazardous under RCRA (either by characteristic or by being listed on the F, K, P, or U lists), assignment of appropriate waste codes, selection of suitable containers and labeling, arrangement of proper storage within accumulation time limits, completion of hazardous waste manifests for shipment, and selection of qualified treatment/disposal facilities. For example, a waste solvent stream from a degreasing operation would be classified as a F001 listed hazardous waste (spent halogenated solvents from degreasing) and also as D001 (characteristic hazardous waste for ignitability if the flash point is below 140 degrees Fahrenheit), requiring both waste codes on the manifest, proper flammable storage during accumulation, and shipment to a permitted incinerator or fuel blending facility.

Beyond routine waste management, safety professionals must also understand their responsibilities under CERCLA (the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act, commonly known as Superfund) when dealing with contaminated sites. CERCLA imposes strict, joint and several, and retroactive liability on potentially responsible parties (PRPs) including current and past owners/operators, generators who arranged for disposal, and transporters who selected the disposal site. Understanding reportable quantities, National Response Center notification requirements, and the relationship between CERCLA and RCRA corrective action is important for both daily practice and the CSP examination.

concepts.title: RCRA Hazardous Waste Characteristics
concepts.description: RCRA defines four characteristics that make a solid waste a hazardous waste, commonly remembered by the mnemonic ICRT. Ignitability (waste code D-001) applies to liquids with a flash point below 140 degrees Fahrenheit (60 degrees Celsius), non-liquids that can cause fire through friction or spontaneous chemical changes, ignitable compressed gases per DOT regulations, and oxidizers. Corrosivity (waste code D-002) applies to aqueous wastes with pH equal to or less than 2.0 (strongly acidic) or pH equal to or greater than 12.5 (strongly alkaline), or liquids that corrode steel at a rate greater than 6.35 mm per year at 55 degrees Celsius. Reactivity (waste code D-003) applies to wastes that are unstable under normal conditions, react violently with water, form potentially explosive mixtures with water, generate toxic gases when mixed with water or acids, contain cyanide or sulfide compounds that generate toxic gases at certain pH levels, are capable of detonation, or are forbidden explosives under DOT rules. Toxicity (waste codes D-004 through D-043) is determined through the Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure (TCLP) test, which simulates leaching in a landfill environment by exposing the waste to an acetic acid solution and measuring the concentration of 40 specific contaminants in the leachate, including 8 metals (arsenic, barium, cadmium, chromium, lead, mercury, selenium, silver) and 32 organic compounds. For example, a waste with a TCLP lead concentration of 5.0 mg/L or higher would be classified as D-008 hazardous waste. On the CSP exam, candidates must know the specific numeric thresholds: flash point below 140 degrees Fahrenheit for ignitability, pH of 2 or below or 12.5 or above for corrosivity, and that TCLP is the test method for the toxicity characteristic.

concepts.title: Universal Waste
concepts.description: Universal waste is a category of hazardous waste subject to streamlined management requirements under 40 CFR 273, designed to encourage proper recycling and management of common wastes that are generated by a wide variety of establishments. The four categories of universal waste are batteries (including nickel-cadmium, lithium, lead-acid, and mercury batteries), pesticides (recalled or collected through waste pesticide collection programs), mercury-containing equipment (such as thermostats, thermometers, barometers, mercury switches, and pressure gauges), and lamps (fluorescent tubes, high-intensity discharge lamps, neon, mercury vapor, and high-pressure sodium lamps that contain mercury). Universal waste handlers are classified as either Small Quantity Handlers (accumulate less than 5,000 kilograms at any time) or Large Quantity Handlers (accumulate 5,000 kilograms or more), with Large Quantity Handlers subject to additional notification and tracking requirements. Key simplified requirements include no manifesting required for shipment (just use a tracking system), containers must be labeled "Universal Waste" with the type (e.g., "Universal Waste - Batteries"), accumulation time limit of one year, and waste must be sent to another universal waste handler, a destination facility, or a foreign destination. For example, a facility that replaces 200 fluorescent tubes per month would manage them as universal waste lamps, labeling collection containers as "Universal Waste - Lamps," storing them intact in protective containers to prevent breakage and mercury release, and shipping them to a lamp recycler within one year. On the CSP exam, know the four categories, that streamlined rules apply, and that the one-year accumulation limit exists for universal waste.

concepts.title: Waste Minimization
concepts.description: Waste minimization is a systematic effort to reduce the volume and toxicity of hazardous waste generated at a facility, and it is a regulatory requirement for all hazardous waste generators under RCRA. The waste minimization hierarchy parallels the broader pollution prevention hierarchy: source reduction is preferred over recycling, which is preferred over treatment to reduce volume or toxicity. Source reduction methods include raw material substitution (replacing a toxic solvent with a less hazardous alternative), process modifications (switching from batch to continuous processing to reduce waste), improved operations (better inventory management to reduce expired chemical disposal), and technology changes (installing closed-loop systems). Recycling methods include on-site recovery of solvents through distillation, reclamation of metals from plating solutions, reuse of rinse water through counter-current systems, and recovery of energy through waste-to-energy processes. For example, an electronics manufacturer might reduce hazardous waste by 60% by substituting a water-based flux for a rosin-based flux in soldering operations (source reduction), recovering and recycling spent solder (recycling), and installing an on-site wastewater treatment system to reduce the volume of hazardous wastewater requiring off-site disposal (treatment). LQGs must certify on each hazardous waste manifest that they have a waste minimization program in place, and RCRA biennial reports must describe waste minimization efforts and results. On the CSP exam, waste minimization is tested in connection with the pollution prevention hierarchy and as a RCRA generator requirement.

concepts.title: Spill Cleanup
concepts.description: Spill cleanup involves the containment, collection, decontamination, and proper disposal of spilled hazardous materials, and the response must follow guidance from the Safety Data Sheet and applicable regulations including RCRA, CERCLA, OSHA HAZWOPER, and DOT requirements. Immediate response priorities include protecting human health and safety (evacuation, PPE for responders), preventing the spill from reaching waterways, drains, or soil, and containing the spread using booms, dikes, absorbents, or physical barriers. Cleanup methods vary by material type: absorbent materials (vermiculite, clay, specialized pads) for liquid spills, neutralization agents for acid or base spills (following SDS guidance), specialized kits for mercury spills, and vacuum systems for large-volume liquid spills. Cleanup materials that are contaminated with hazardous substances may themselves become hazardous waste requiring proper characterization, labeling, and disposal. Under CERCLA, any release of a hazardous substance at or above its Reportable Quantity (RQ) must be reported to the National Response Center (NRC) at 1-800-424-8802 immediately, and failure to report can result in significant penalties. For example, if a forklift punctures a drum of toluene (RQ = 1,000 pounds) and the entire 55-gallon drum (approximately 380 pounds) is released, this would not trigger CERCLA reporting because the release is below the RQ, but it would still require proper cleanup under RCRA and potentially trigger state reporting requirements. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand the relationship between spill response, CERCLA reporting, and the determination of whether cleanup materials become hazardous waste.

concepts.title: Disposal Methods
concepts.description: Hazardous waste disposal encompasses several technologies selected based on waste characteristics, regulatory requirements, volume, cost, and environmental considerations. Incineration involves thermal destruction at high temperatures (typically 1,800-2,200 degrees Fahrenheit for hazardous waste) in specially designed and permitted facilities, achieving destruction and removal efficiency (DRE) of 99.99% for most hazardous wastes and 99.9999% (the "six nines" rule) for dioxin-containing wastes, with residual ash typically landfilled. Secure chemical landfills are engineered containment facilities with double liner systems (typically a composite liner of clay and synthetic membrane), leachate collection and removal systems, groundwater monitoring wells, and run-on/run-off controls, designed to isolate hazardous waste from the environment indefinitely. Deep well injection involves injecting liquid hazardous waste into geologically stable formations thousands of feet below underground sources of drinking water, through wells constructed with multiple layers of casing and cement, with continuous monitoring of injection pressure and annular pressure. Chemical treatment includes neutralization of acids and bases, oxidation/reduction reactions to convert hazardous constituents to less hazardous forms, and precipitation of dissolved metals into insoluble forms for separation and disposal. Solidification and stabilization involves mixing waste with binding agents such as Portland cement, pozzolanic materials, or thermoplastic binders to physically contain hazardous constituents and reduce leachability. For example, soil contaminated with lead from a former firing range might be stabilized with Portland cement and phosphate additives to reduce lead leachability below TCLP limits, then disposed of as non-hazardous waste. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand the basic principles of each disposal method and know which methods are appropriate for different waste types.

concepts.title: Hazardous Waste Manifest System
concepts.description: The Uniform Hazardous Waste Manifest (EPA Form 8700-22) is the key document in the RCRA cradle-to-grave tracking system, accompanying hazardous waste from the point of generation through transportation to the final treatment, storage, or disposal facility (TSDF). The generator initiates the manifest by providing detailed information including generator name and EPA ID number, transporter name and EPA ID number, designated receiving facility and its EPA ID number, US DOT description of the waste (proper shipping name, hazard class, UN/NA number, packing group), waste quantities, applicable waste codes, special handling instructions, and generator certification. The manifest is a multi-copy document: the generator retains one copy at the time of shipment, each transporter signs and retains a copy upon accepting and delivering the waste, and the designated facility signs and returns a copy to the generator within 30 days to confirm receipt. If the generator does not receive a signed copy back from the TSDF within 35 days, the generator must contact the transporter and TSDF to determine the status of the waste. If the signed manifest is not received within 45 days of the date of shipment, the generator must file an Exception Report with EPA or the authorized state agency. The e-Manifest system, implemented by EPA in 2018, provides an electronic option for manifest submission and tracking, though paper manifests are still accepted. Generators must retain copies of all manifests for at least three years from the date of shipment. For the CSP exam, the critical numbers to remember are the 35-day follow-up threshold and the 45-day exception report deadline, as well as the three-year record retention requirement.

concepts.title: CERCLA/Superfund
concepts.description: The Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA), commonly known as Superfund, was enacted in 1980 to address the cleanup of sites contaminated with hazardous substances and provides the legal framework for identifying contaminated sites, evaluating cleanup alternatives, and compelling responsible parties to pay for cleanup. CERCLA establishes four categories of Potentially Responsible Parties (PRPs): current owners and operators of the facility, past owners and operators at the time of disposal, persons who arranged for disposal or treatment of hazardous substances at the facility (generators), and transporters who selected the site for disposal. Liability under CERCLA is strict (no negligence required), joint and several (any one PRP can be held liable for the entire cleanup cost), and retroactive (applies to actions taken before the law was enacted). Reportable Quantities (RQs) are established for each hazardous substance, and any release at or above the RQ must be reported immediately to the National Response Center (NRC) at 1-800-424-8802. The Superfund cleanup process follows the National Contingency Plan (NCP) and includes preliminary assessment/site investigation, listing on the National Priorities List (NPL) for the most contaminated sites, remedial investigation/feasibility study (RI/FS), selection of a remedy documented in a Record of Decision (ROD), remedial design/remedial action (RD/RA), and long-term monitoring. For example, a former industrial site where multiple companies disposed of solvents over 40 years might be listed on the NPL, with EPA or the state agency pursuing all identifiable PRPs to share cleanup costs that could reach tens of millions of dollars, even though some of the disposal activities occurred decades ago and were legal at the time. On the CSP exam, focus on the liability framework (strict, joint and several, retroactive), the four categories of PRPs, and the RQ/NRC notification requirement.

concepts.title: Remediation
concepts.description: Remediation refers to the cleanup and restoration of contaminated soil, groundwater, and other environmental media at sites where hazardous substances have been released, using various technologies selected based on the type and extent of contamination, site geology and hydrogeology, cleanup goals, cost, and time constraints. Excavation and off-site disposal is the most straightforward approach, involving physically removing contaminated soil and transporting it to a permitted landfill or treatment facility, but it can be very expensive for large volumes and may not be feasible for deep contamination or contaminated groundwater. Soil Vapor Extraction (SVE) removes volatile organic compounds from the unsaturated zone by applying vacuum to extraction wells, drawing contaminated air through the soil matrix to the surface where it is treated (typically with activated carbon or thermal oxidation) before discharge, and is highly effective for volatile contaminants like benzene, toluene, and trichloroethylene in permeable soils. Bioremediation uses naturally occurring or enhanced microbial populations to degrade organic contaminants into harmless products such as carbon dioxide and water, and can be implemented in-situ (treating contamination in place) or ex-situ (excavating soil and treating it in biopiles, landfarming, or bioreactors), with typical treatment times of months to years depending on conditions. Pump and treat systems extract contaminated groundwater through wells, treat it above ground using technologies such as air stripping, activated carbon adsorption, or chemical oxidation, and either discharge treated water to surface water under NPDES permits or reinject it into the aquifer. In-situ chemical oxidation (ISCO) involves injecting oxidizing agents such as permanganate, persulfate, hydrogen peroxide (Fenton reagent), or ozone directly into contaminated soil or groundwater to chemically destroy organic contaminants in place. Monitored natural attenuation (MNA) relies on natural processes including biodegradation, dispersion, dilution, and volatilization to reduce contaminant concentrations over time, with regular monitoring to verify that natural processes are achieving cleanup goals within acceptable timeframes. For the CSP exam, candidates should understand the basic principles of each remediation technology and be able to match appropriate technologies to given contamination scenarios.

diagrams.title: Hazardous Waste Manifest System (Cradle-to-Grave)
steps: GENERATOR - Identifies waste, completes manifest, selects TSDF
TRANSPORTER - Signs manifest, transports to designated facility
TREATMENT/STORAGE/DISPOSAL FACILITY (TSDF) - Receives waste, signs manifest, sends copy back
If YES: File manifest, retain 3 years
If NO: File Exception Report with EPA regional office

mnemonics.title: RCRA Hazardous Waste Characteristics
mnemonics.description: Ignitability (D-001, flash point <140°F), Corrosivity (D-002, pH ≤2 or ≥12.5), Reactivity (D-003, unstable/explosive/toxic gas generation), Toxicity (D-004 through D-043, fails TCLP test for 40 specific contaminants including 8 metals and 32 organics).

mnemonics.title: Universal Waste Types
mnemonics.description: Batteries, Pesticides, Lamps (fluorescent/HID/mercury), Mercury-containing equipment (thermostats, switches). Remember "Bad Pesticides Light Mercury" to recall all four categories.

mnemonics.title: Four Categories of Potentially Responsible Parties
mnemonics.description: Current Owners/Operators, Past Owners/Operators (at time of disposal), Generators (arranged for disposal), Transporters (who selected the disposal site). Liability is Strict, Joint and Several, and Retroactive.

examTips: Universal wastes: Batteries, Pesticides, Mercury-containing equipment, Lamps. Streamlined rules with one-year accumulation limit and no manifest required for shipment.
RCRA characteristics (ICRT): Ignitability (D-001, FP <140°F), Corrosivity (D-002, pH ≤2 or ≥12.5), Reactivity (D-003), Toxicity (D-004+, determined by TCLP test). Know the waste codes.
Hazardous waste manifest (EPA Form 8700-22): generator must file Exception Report with EPA if signed manifest not returned within 45 days. Records retained 3 years minimum.
CERCLA/Superfund liability is STRICT (no negligence needed), JOINT AND SEVERAL (any PRP can pay the full cost), and RETROACTIVE (applies to past disposal). Know all four PRP categories.
Reportable Quantity (RQ) releases must be reported IMMEDIATELY to the National Response Center (NRC) at 1-800-424-8802. Failure to report carries significant penalties.
Hazardous waste incineration requires 99.99% DRE (four nines) for most wastes and 99.9999% (six nines) for dioxin-bearing wastes.

topicContent.overview:
Sustainability in the EHS context means meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs, a concept first articulated in the 1987 Brundtland Commission report "Our Common Future." It encompasses environmental stewardship, social responsibility, and economic viability, often referred to as the "triple bottom line" framework of People, Planet, and Profit. For safety professionals, sustainability is increasingly integrated into organizational strategy and operations, with EHS performance serving as a key indicator of sustainability commitment. The CSP exam includes questions on sustainability concepts, the waste hierarchy, environmental management systems (ISO 14001), LEED green building certification, life cycle assessment, and the relationship between EHS and ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) reporting frameworks.

In practice, a safety professional contributes to organizational sustainability through waste minimization programs, energy efficiency improvements, pollution prevention initiatives, green chemistry adoption, sustainable procurement practices, and worker health and well-being programs. For example, a manufacturing facility might implement a comprehensive sustainability program that reduces hazardous waste generation by 40% through solvent substitution and closed-loop recycling (environmental), decreases workplace injuries by 50% through ergonomic improvements and safety culture initiatives (social), and saves $2 million annually through reduced raw material costs, lower disposal fees, and decreased workers compensation claims (economic). These interconnections between environmental protection, worker safety, and business performance illustrate why sustainability is now a core competency for safety professionals.

Environmental Management Systems (EMS), particularly those conforming to the ISO 14001 standard, provide the structured framework through which organizations implement sustainability goals. ISO 14001 follows the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle and requires organizations to establish an environmental policy, identify environmental aspects and impacts, set objectives and targets, implement operational controls, monitor and measure performance, and continually improve. Many organizations also pursue LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) certification for their buildings and facilities, integrating sustainable design and construction practices that benefit both the environment and occupant health and safety. Understanding these frameworks and their connection to overall organizational sustainability strategy is increasingly important for the CSP examination.

concepts.title: Triple Bottom Line
concepts.description: The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework, coined by John Elkington in 1994, evaluates organizational performance across three dimensions: People (social), Planet (environmental), and Profit (economic). The social dimension encompasses worker health and safety, fair labor practices, community engagement, human rights, diversity and inclusion, and product safety. The environmental dimension includes pollution prevention, resource conservation, biodiversity protection, waste minimization, carbon footprint reduction, and compliance with environmental regulations. The economic dimension goes beyond traditional profit metrics to include long-term economic sustainability, risk management, innovation investment, supply chain resilience, and creation of shared value for stakeholders. For example, a company that invests in ergonomic workstation design (People) that reduces repetitive strain injuries by 60%, also decreases waste from defective products by 25% (Planet) because comfortable workers make fewer errors, and saves $500,000 annually in reduced workers compensation costs and rework expenses (Profit). On the CSP exam, candidates should understand that true sustainability requires balancing all three dimensions, that EHS performance directly contributes to the People and Planet pillars, and that safety investments typically generate positive returns across all three dimensions through reduced incidents, lower liability, improved productivity, and enhanced reputation.

concepts.title: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
concepts.description: The waste management hierarchy of Reduce, Reuse, Recycle (plus Recovery and Disposal) establishes a preferred order of waste management approaches, with waste reduction at the source being the most environmentally preferable and disposal being the least preferred option. Reduce (source reduction) involves preventing waste generation entirely through product redesign, process modification, material substitution, and operational efficiency improvements, and is always the most preferred approach because it avoids all downstream costs and environmental impacts. Reuse involves using materials again for their original purpose or a similar purpose without significant processing, such as refilling containers, reconditioning pallets, or repurposing equipment, extending the useful life of products and materials. Recycling involves collecting, processing, and converting waste materials into new products or raw materials, such as recycling scrap metal into new steel products, recovering solvents through distillation, or processing used paper into new paper products. Recovery involves extracting energy or materials from waste that cannot be recycled, such as waste-to-energy incineration that generates electricity from combustible waste streams. Disposal, including landfilling and non-energy-recovery incineration, is the least preferred option and should only be used when all higher-tier options have been exhausted. For example, a food manufacturing company might reduce packaging material by 30% through design optimization (reduce), implement a returnable container program with major customers (reuse), recycle cardboard and plastic wrap through material recovery programs (recycle), send food processing waste to an anaerobic digester to generate biogas (recovery), and only landfill truly residual waste that has no other management option (disposal). On the CSP exam, candidates should know the complete hierarchy and understand that source reduction is always preferred over end-of-life management approaches.

concepts.title: Supply Chain Sustainability
concepts.description: Supply chain sustainability extends an organization environmental and social responsibility upstream to suppliers and downstream to customers and end-of-life product management, recognizing that an organization environmental and social footprint extends far beyond its own facility boundaries. Key elements include green procurement policies that require suppliers to meet environmental and safety standards, supplier audits and assessments that evaluate environmental compliance, labor practices, and safety performance, responsible sourcing of raw materials to avoid conflict minerals and deforestation-linked commodities, and product stewardship programs that manage environmental impacts throughout the product lifecycle. Life cycle thinking is fundamental, requiring organizations to consider raw material extraction impacts, manufacturing energy and emissions, transportation fuel consumption and emissions, product use phase impacts, and end-of-life disposal or recycling. For example, a major retailer might implement a supply chain sustainability program that requires all suppliers to have ISO 14001 certified environmental management systems, conducts annual safety and environmental audits at key supplier facilities, mandates responsible sourcing of cotton and palm oil, tracks Scope 3 greenhouse gas emissions throughout the supply chain, and designs products for recyclability. On the CSP exam, supply chain sustainability may be tested in the context of organizational environmental management responsibilities and the expanding scope of EHS professional practice to include upstream and downstream impacts beyond the facility fence line.

concepts.title: ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)
concepts.description: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) is a framework used by investors, regulators, and stakeholders to evaluate an organization sustainability performance and risk exposure, and it has become one of the most significant business trends affecting EHS professionals. The Environmental pillar includes climate change mitigation and adaptation, greenhouse gas emissions, energy efficiency, water management, waste and pollution, biodiversity, and environmental compliance. The Social pillar encompasses occupational health and safety performance, workforce diversity and inclusion, human capital development, community relations, human rights, supply chain labor standards, and product safety. The Governance pillar addresses board structure and independence, executive compensation, risk management frameworks, business ethics, regulatory compliance, transparency, and anti-corruption measures. EHS professionals play a central role in ESG because safety metrics (TRIR, DART, fatality rates) are key Social indicators, environmental compliance and pollution prevention directly feed the Environmental pillar, and EHS management systems contribute to Governance through systematic risk management. Major ESG reporting frameworks include the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI), the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB), the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), and the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB). For example, a publicly traded chemical company might report its ESG performance including Scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions, water consumption per unit of production, Total Recordable Incident Rate, process safety event rate, workforce diversity metrics, and board-level ESG oversight structure. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand how EHS performance connects directly to ESG reporting and that ESG is an increasingly tested topic area.

concepts.title: Life Cycle Assessment
concepts.description: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a systematic methodology for evaluating the environmental impacts of a product, process, or service throughout its entire life cycle from raw material extraction through manufacturing, distribution, use, and end-of-life management, often described as a "cradle-to-grave" analysis. The LCA process, standardized by ISO 14040 and ISO 14044, involves four phases: goal and scope definition (establishing the purpose, system boundaries, and functional unit), life cycle inventory analysis (quantifying all inputs and outputs including raw materials, energy, emissions, and waste at each life cycle stage), life cycle impact assessment (evaluating the significance of environmental impacts in categories such as global warming potential, ozone depletion, acidification, eutrophication, and resource depletion), and interpretation (analyzing results, identifying significant issues, and drawing conclusions and recommendations). A "cradle-to-gate" analysis covers only the stages up to the factory gate, while a "cradle-to-cradle" analysis considers complete material recycling in a closed-loop system. For example, an LCA of a beverage container might compare glass bottles, aluminum cans, and PET plastic bottles across all life cycle stages, revealing that glass has the highest transportation impact due to weight, aluminum has the highest raw material extraction impact but the best recycling profile, and PET has the lowest production energy but problematic end-of-life management. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand the four phases of LCA, the difference between cradle-to-grave and cradle-to-gate boundaries, and how LCA supports environmental decision-making.

concepts.title: LEED Certification
concepts.description: Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED) is the most widely used green building certification system in the world, developed and administered by the U.S. Green Building Council (USGBC), that rates buildings and communities on their environmental performance across multiple categories. LEED v4.1 evaluates buildings across credit categories including Integrative Process, Location and Transportation, Sustainable Sites, Water Efficiency, Energy and Atmosphere, Materials and Resources, Indoor Environmental Quality, Innovation, and Regional Priority. Projects earn points in each category, with four certification levels based on total points achieved out of 110 possible: Certified (40-49 points), Silver (50-59 points), Gold (60-79 points), and Platinum (80+ points). The Indoor Environmental Quality category is particularly relevant to safety professionals, addressing ventilation effectiveness, thermal comfort, interior lighting, daylight access, acoustic performance, and low-emitting materials. For example, a company building a new corporate headquarters might pursue LEED Gold certification by incorporating energy-efficient HVAC systems with enhanced filtration and outdoor air delivery (improving both energy performance and indoor air quality for occupants), using low-VOC paints, adhesives, and furniture to reduce indoor air pollutant exposure, installing daylighting controls to optimize natural light while reducing energy consumption, and implementing water-efficient fixtures that reduce potable water use by 40%. On the CSP exam, candidates should know the four LEED certification levels (Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum), that LEED is administered by the USGBC, and that Indoor Environmental Quality is a key category connecting green building design to occupant health and safety.

concepts.title: ISO 14001 Environmental Management System
concepts.description: ISO 14001 is the international standard for Environmental Management Systems (EMS), providing a framework for organizations to systematically manage their environmental responsibilities in a way that contributes to the environmental pillar of sustainability while also supporting business objectives. The standard follows the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle: Plan involves establishing an environmental policy, identifying environmental aspects and their impacts, determining legal and other requirements, setting environmental objectives and targets, and developing programs to achieve them. Do involves implementing the planned actions through operational controls, training, communication, documentation, and emergency preparedness. Check involves monitoring and measuring environmental performance, evaluating compliance with legal requirements, conducting internal audits, and reporting results to management. Act involves conducting management review, addressing nonconformities, and identifying opportunities for continual improvement. Key requirements include top management commitment and leadership, identification of significant environmental aspects (activities, products, or services that interact with the environment), a life cycle perspective in managing environmental aspects, competence and awareness of personnel, documented information controls, operational planning and control, and performance evaluation. For example, a paint manufacturing company with ISO 14001 certification might identify VOC emissions, wastewater discharge, hazardous waste generation, and energy consumption as its significant environmental aspects, then establish objectives to reduce VOC emissions by 25% through reformulation, decrease wastewater volume by 30% through water recycling, and improve energy efficiency by 15% through equipment upgrades, with regular monitoring, internal auditing, and management review to drive continual improvement. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand that ISO 14001 follows the PDCA cycle, requires identification of environmental aspects and impacts, mandates a commitment to continual improvement, and is compatible with other management system standards such as ISO 45001 for occupational health and safety.

concepts.title: Carbon Footprint and Greenhouse Gas Management
concepts.description: A carbon footprint measures the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions associated with an organization, product, event, or individual, expressed in metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e) to allow comparison of different greenhouse gases based on their global warming potential. The GHG Protocol, the most widely used international accounting standard, classifies emissions into three scopes: Scope 1 (direct emissions from owned or controlled sources such as on-site combustion, company vehicles, and fugitive emissions from equipment), Scope 2 (indirect emissions from purchased electricity, steam, heating, and cooling), and Scope 3 (all other indirect emissions in the value chain including business travel, employee commuting, purchased goods and services, waste disposal, and product use and end-of-life). The six main greenhouse gases covered by the Kyoto Protocol and tracked in corporate inventories are carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), perfluorocarbons (PFCs), and sulfur hexafluoride (SF6), each with different global warming potentials relative to CO2. For example, a manufacturing facility might calculate its carbon footprint as 50,000 metric tons CO2e per year, comprising 15,000 tons from Scope 1 (natural gas combustion and vehicle fleet), 25,000 tons from Scope 2 (purchased electricity), and 10,000 tons from measurable Scope 3 categories (business travel, waste disposal, employee commuting), then set targets to reduce its total footprint by 30% by 2030 through energy efficiency improvements, renewable energy procurement, fleet electrification, and waste reduction. On the CSP exam, candidates should understand the three scopes of emissions, the concept of CO2 equivalent, and how carbon footprint management connects to broader organizational sustainability and ESG reporting frameworks.

diagrams.title: Waste Management Hierarchy (Most to Least Preferred)
ESG Framework - Three Pillars
rows: Carbon emissions, waste reduction, water use, pollution prevention
EHS programs directly impact environmental performance
Worker safety (TCIR/DART), community health, diversity, human rights
Safety metrics are core Social indicators
Board oversight, ethics, transparency, regulatory compliance

mnemonics.title: 3 Ps of Sustainability
mnemonics.description: People (social: worker safety, community, fair labor), Planet (environmental: pollution, resources, waste), Profit (economic: long-term viability, risk reduction, shared value). All three must be balanced for true sustainability. EHS directly supports People and Planet.

mnemonics.title: LEED Certification Levels
mnemonics.description: Certified (40-49 pts), Silver (50-59 pts), Gold (60-79 pts), Platinum (80+ pts) out of 110 possible points. Remember the increasing thresholds by thinking of precious metals getting progressively rarer and more valuable.

mnemonics.title: ISO 14001 EMS Cycle
mnemonics.description: Plan (policy, aspects, objectives) - Do (implement, train, operate) - Check (monitor, audit, review) - Act (correct, improve). Same cycle used in ISO 45001 OH&S management systems. Drives continual improvement.

examTips: Sustainability = meeting present needs without compromising future generations (Brundtland Commission 1987). Know the triple bottom line: People, Planet, Profit.
Waste hierarchy: Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Recovery > Disposal. Source reduction is ALWAYS the most preferred approach because it prevents waste generation entirely.
ESG is increasingly tested on the CSP exam. EHS performance connects directly to Environmental (compliance, emissions) and Social (safety metrics like TRIR, DART) pillars.
LEED certification levels: Certified (40-49), Silver (50-59), Gold (60-79), Platinum (80+) out of 110 points. Administered by USGBC. Indoor Environmental Quality is key for safety professionals.
ISO 14001 EMS follows Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle. Requires environmental policy, identification of environmental aspects/impacts, objectives and targets, and commitment to continual improvement.
GHG Protocol Scopes: Scope 1 = direct emissions (owned sources), Scope 2 = indirect from purchased energy, Scope 3 = all other value chain emissions. Know the three scopes for ESG reporting.

topicContent.overview:
Understanding the impact of environmental issues is essential for safety professionals as environmental hazards intersect with occupational health, regulatory compliance, community safety, and organizational risk management. Environmental impacts range from legacy contamination and aging infrastructure concerns (lead paint, asbestos, underground storage tanks) to emerging challenges including climate change adaptation, air quality management under the Clean Air Act, water quality protection under the Clean Water Act, and the growing importance of ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) reporting to stakeholders. For the CSP exam, candidates must understand NAAQS criteria pollutants, asbestos regulations and exposure limits, the basics of the Clean Air Act and Clean Water Act, and how climate change affects safety management.

In real-world practice, environmental impact assessment requires safety professionals to evaluate both the direct effects of workplace activities on the environment and the effects of environmental conditions on worker health and safety. For example, a safety professional at a power generation facility must manage air emissions compliance under the Clean Air Act (including NAAQS criteria pollutants, hazardous air pollutants, and greenhouse gases), water discharge permits under the Clean Water Act, asbestos management in older plant buildings, heat stress prevention programs that are increasingly important as climate change drives higher temperatures, and emergency preparedness plans that account for more frequent and severe weather events. The ability to integrate environmental compliance with occupational safety management creates value for organizations and is a distinguishing competency of the CSP-certified professional.

Emerging environmental issues are increasingly prominent on the CSP exam, reflecting the expanding role of safety professionals in addressing climate change impacts, ESG reporting demands, and the concept of bioaccumulation of persistent pollutants in ecosystems and food chains. Safety professionals must understand how environmental regulatory frameworks (Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, RCRA, CERCLA) interact with occupational safety regulations (OSHA standards) and how environmental conditions such as extreme heat, poor air quality from wildfire smoke, and flood risks affect workplace safety planning.
