calculations.problem: A system has: Stage 1 (two parallel components, R=0.85 each), then Stage 2 (single component R=0.95) in series. Calculate overall system reliability.
calculations.interpretation: Combined series-parallel reliability is 0.929. The parallel stage improved from 0.85 to 0.978, but series with 0.95 brings it down.
calculations.examTip: Solve parallel subsystems first, then combine in series.

calculations.title: Safety Program ROI
calculations.problem: A company invested $150,000 in a safety program. The program saved $450,000 in reduced WC costs, decreased absenteeism, and improved productivity. Calculate the ROI.
calculations.interpretation: ROI of 200% means for every $1 invested, the company got $2 in net benefit (or $3 total return).
calculations.examTip: Include all direct and indirect savings: WC premiums, lost workdays, training costs, productivity gains.

calculations.title: Cost-Benefit Ratio
calculations.problem: Machine guarding upgrade costs $25,000 to install plus $3,000/year maintenance. Expected to prevent 2 hand injuries per year (average cost $40,000 each). Calculate 5-year benefit-cost ratio.
calculations.interpretation: Benefit-cost ratio of 10:1 strongly justifies the investment.
calculations.examTip: BCR > 1.0 means benefits outweigh costs. Higher is better. Include both direct and indirect injury costs.

calculations.title: Future Value of Safety Investment
calculations.problem: A company invests $100,000 in safety improvements expected to save $30,000 per year. If money earns 5% annually, what is the future value of savings after 5 years?
calculations.interpretation: The $30,000 annual savings accumulates to $165,769 over 5 years including interest.
calculations.examTip: Future value of annuity formula accounts for the time value of money.

calculations.title: Present Value of Future Savings
calculations.problem: A safety system will save $50,000 per year for 4 years. Using a discount rate of 8%, calculate the present value of these savings.
calculations.interpretation: The present value of $50,000/yr for 4 years at 8% discount is about $165,605.
calculations.examTip: If PV of benefits exceeds the investment cost, the project has a positive NPV.

calculations.title: Ideal Gas Law - Pressure Change
calculations.problem: A sealed container holds gas at 1 atm and 20C (293K). If heated to 80C (353K) with constant volume, what is the new pressure?
calculations.interpretation: Pressure increases about 20% due to heating. Important for PSM and sealed container hazards.
calculations.examTip: Always use absolute temperature (Kelvin) in gas law calculations. K = C + 273.

calculations.title: Ventilation Flow Rate
calculations.problem: A rectangular duct is 2 ft wide and 1.5 ft tall. Air velocity is measured at 2,000 ft/min. Calculate the volumetric flow rate.
calculations.interpretation: 6,000 cfm is the volume of air moved through the duct per minute.
calculations.examTip: Q = AV is fundamental. Units must match: ft2 x ft/min = ft3/min (cfm).

calculations.title: Secondary Containment Volume
calculations.problem: A storage area has one 10,000-gallon tank and two 5,000-gallon tanks. SPCC requires containment for the largest single tank plus sufficient freeboard. What is the minimum containment volume required?
calculations.formula: Min Volume = Largest Tank Volume (plus freeboard)
calculations.interpretation: The secondary containment must hold at least 10,000 gallons (the largest single container) per SPCC 40 CFR 112.
calculations.examTip: SPCC rule: containment must hold the largest single container. Some jurisdictions require 110% of largest.

criticalNumbers.label: Standard guardrail height (top rail)
criticalNumbers.memory: Think of a 3.5-foot kitchen counter - guardrails are about counter height

criticalNumbers.label: General industry fall protection trigger height
criticalNumbers.memory: Average person height = 6 ft, fall from your own height = danger

criticalNumbers.label: Construction fall protection trigger height
criticalNumbers.memory: Same as general industry - 6 ft for construction too

criticalNumbers.label: Minimum toeboard height
criticalNumbers.memory: 4 fingers stacked = ~4 inches for toeboard

criticalNumbers.label: Trench depth requiring protective system
criticalNumbers.memory: High-5 = 5 ft deep trench needs shoring

criticalNumbers.label: Max travel distance to Class A extinguisher
criticalNumbers.memory: 75 ft = about 25 paces to reach Class A fire ext

criticalNumbers.label: Max travel distance to Class B extinguisher
criticalNumbers.memory: 50 ft for flammable liquids (B) - shorter because more dangerous

criticalNumbers.label: Travel time to eyewash/shower station
criticalNumbers.memory: 10-second rule: count to 10 walking to the eyewash

criticalNumbers.label: Ladder base distance to height ratio (75.5 deg)
criticalNumbers.memory: For every 4 ft up, 1 ft out from the wall

criticalNumbers.label: Ladder extension above landing surface
criticalNumbers.memory: 3 rungs above the roof edge = about 3 ft

criticalNumbers.label: Max span for scaffold plank (light duty)
criticalNumbers.memory: 10 ft plank span for scaffold = a parking space length

criticalNumbers.label: Horizontal entry distance for non-entry rescue
criticalNumbers.memory: 35 ft = about the width of a 2-lane road with sidewalks

criticalNumbers.label: OSHA 300 log retention period
criticalNumbers.memory: Keep logs for a 5-year plan cycle

criticalNumbers.label: OSHA 300A Summary posting period
criticalNumbers.memory: February through April = tax season = posting season

criticalNumbers.label: Employee exposure/medical record retention
criticalNumbers.memory: 30 years = an entire career of exposure records

criticalNumbers.label: Time to report workplace fatality to OSHA
criticalNumbers.memory: 8 hours = one work shift to report a death

criticalNumbers.label: Time to report hospitalization/amputation/eye loss
criticalNumbers.memory: 24 hours = one full day to report serious injury

criticalNumbers.label: Time to contest OSHA citation
criticalNumbers.memory: 15 working days = 3 calendar weeks to contest

criticalNumbers.label: Typical abatement period for serious violations
criticalNumbers.memory: 90 days = one quarter to fix serious problems

criticalNumbers.label: PSM Process Hazard Analysis revalidation
criticalNumbers.memory: PHA every 5 years, same as log retention

criticalNumbers.label: Hazardous waste accumulation time (large generator)
criticalNumbers.memory: 90 days = one quarter to ship hazardous waste

criticalNumbers.label: Hazardous waste accumulation time (small generator)
criticalNumbers.memory: 270 days = 9 months, like a pregnancy for small gen waste

criticalNumbers.label: Time to establish audiometric baseline after hire
criticalNumbers.memory: 6 months = half a year to get baseline hearing test

criticalNumbers.label: Frequency of annual audiometric testing
criticalNumbers.memory: Annual = once per year hearing check

criticalNumbers.label: Respirator fit test frequency
criticalNumbers.memory: Annual fit test, same as hearing test

criticalNumbers.label: Quiet period before baseline audiogram
criticalNumbers.memory: 14 hours away from noise before baseline test

criticalNumbers.label: Minimum oxygen level (oxygen-deficient atmosphere)
criticalNumbers.memory: Normal is 20.9%, minus 1.4% = 19.5% is low danger zone

criticalNumbers.label: Maximum oxygen level (oxygen-enriched atmosphere)
criticalNumbers.memory: Normal is 20.9%, plus 2.6% = 23.5% is fire risk

criticalNumbers.label: Action level for combustible gas in confined space
criticalNumbers.memory: 10% of the LEL = time to evacuate, only 1/10 to boom

criticalNumbers.label: OSHA noise PEL (8-hour TWA)
criticalNumbers.memory: 90 dBA PEL: think "90 = OSHA Permissible"

criticalNumbers.label: OSHA noise action level (hearing conservation trigger)
criticalNumbers.memory: 85 = Action Level, 5 less than PEL of 90

criticalNumbers.label: OSHA noise exchange rate (doubling rate)
criticalNumbers.memory: OSHA uses 5 dB exchange rate (NIOSH uses 3 dB)

criticalNumbers.label: NIOSH recommended noise exchange rate
criticalNumbers.memory: NIOSH 3 dB is more protective than OSHA 5 dB

criticalNumbers.label: Asbestos PEL (fibers per cubic centimeter)
criticalNumbers.memory: 0.1 f/cc = one-tenth of a fiber per cc for asbestos

criticalNumbers.label: Respirable crystalline silica PEL
criticalNumbers.memory: 50 micrograms per cubic meter for silica dust

criticalNumbers.label: Lead PEL (micrograms per cubic meter)
criticalNumbers.memory: Lead PEL = same as silica PEL = 50 ug/m3

criticalNumbers.label: Lead action level
criticalNumbers.memory: Lead action level at 30, PEL at 50

criticalNumbers.label: Benzene PEL (8-hour TWA)
criticalNumbers.memory: Benzene = 1 ppm, the simplest PEL to remember

criticalNumbers.label: Carbon monoxide PEL
criticalNumbers.memory: CO PEL = 50 ppm, same number as lead/silica ug/m3

criticalNumbers.label: Hydrogen sulfide ceiling limit
criticalNumbers.memory: H2S ceiling at 20 ppm - rotten eggs smell danger

criticalNumbers.label: EPCRA Tier II reporting threshold (most chemicals)
criticalNumbers.memory: 10,000 lbs = 5 tons for community right-to-know reporting

criticalNumbers.label: PSM threshold quantity for chlorine
criticalNumbers.memory: Chlorine TQ: 2,500 lbs for PSM coverage

criticalNumbers.label: SPCC aggregate above-ground oil storage threshold
criticalNumbers.memory: 1,320 gal = think of a year (12 months) + 20 bonus

criticalNumbers.label: CERCLA reportable quantity range
criticalNumbers.memory: RQ varies by chemical from 1 lb to 5,000 lbs

criticalNumbers.label: DOT small quantity exemption threshold
criticalNumbers.memory: 1,000 kg = 1 metric ton for DOT small quantity

criticalNumbers.label: Forklift capacity - always check the data plate
criticalNumbers.memory: Never guess forklift capacity - read the data plate

criticalNumbers.label: OSHA incidence rate base (hours worked per 100 FT workers/year)
criticalNumbers.memory: 200,000 = 100 workers x 40 hrs x 50 weeks

criticalNumbers.label: Severity rate multiplier (same base as incidence)
criticalNumbers.memory: Same 200,000 base for both incidence and severity rates

criticalNumbers.label: Molar volume at 25C and 1 atm (liters/mole)
criticalNumbers.memory: 24.45 L/mol at standard conditions for ppm-mg/m3 conversion

criticalNumbers.label: NIOSH Lifting Equation load constant (LC)
criticalNumbers.memory: 51 lbs = max recommended weight under ideal conditions

criticalNumbers.label: NIOSH Lifting Equation load constant (metric)
criticalNumbers.memory: 23 kg = 51 lbs in metric, the ideal lift limit

criticalNumbers.label: Typical duct transport velocity for dust
criticalNumbers.memory: 4,000 fpm = typical duct velocity to keep dust moving

criticalNumbers.label: Capture velocity for low-toxicity dust at quiet air
criticalNumbers.memory: 100-200 fpm capture velocity for dust in still air

commonExamQuestions: What do the colors represent?

formulas.name: Total Case Incident Rate (TCIR)
formulas.description: Calculates the number of recordable injuries and illnesses per 100 full-time employees per year. Used to compare safety performance across companies or industries regardless of size. The 200,000 factor represents the hours worked by 100 employees working 40 hours per week for 50 weeks.

variables.name: Number of Recordable Cases
variables.description: Total number of OSHA-recordable injuries and illnesses during the period

variables.name: Employee Hours Worked
variables.description: Total hours worked by all employees during the period

variables.name: Standardization Factor
variables.description: Equivalent of 100 full-time employees working 2,000 hours/year

workedExample.problem: A manufacturing plant had 12 recordable injuries in one year. Employees worked a total of 500,000 hours. Calculate the TCIR.
steps: Step 1: Identify the values. N = 12 recordable cases, EH = 500,000 hours worked.
Step 2: Apply the formula. TCIR = (N x 200,000) / EH
workedExample.answer: TCIR = 4.8 recordable cases per 100 full-time employees per year

formulas.examTip: This is one of the most commonly tested formulas on the CSP exam. Remember to use 200,000 as the constant. Questions may give hours in different units or ask you to compare two facilities. Make sure you include ALL recordable cases (injuries and illnesses).

formulas.name: DART Rate (Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred)
formulas.description: Measures the rate of injuries and illnesses serious enough to cause days away from work, restricted work activity, or job transfer per 100 full-time employees. A subset of TCIR that focuses on more severe cases. This is the rate most commonly used by OSHA for targeting inspections.

variables.name: Number of DART Cases
variables.description: Number of cases involving days away, restricted duty, or transfer

variables.name: Employee Hours Worked
variables.description: Total hours worked by all employees during the period

variables.name: Standardization Factor
variables.description: Equivalent of 100 full-time employees working 2,000 hours/year

workedExample.problem: A company had 8 DART cases out of 15 total recordable cases. Employees worked 400,000 hours. Calculate both the TCIR and the DART rate.
steps: Step 1: For TCIR, use all 15 recordable cases. TCIR = (15 x 200,000) / 400,000 = 7.5
Step 2: For DART, use only the 8 DART cases.
workedExample.answer: DART Rate = 4.0 cases per 100 full-time employees. Note that DART (4.0) is always less than or equal to TCIR (7.5) since DART cases are a subset.

formulas.examTip: The exam may try to confuse you by providing total recordable cases and asking for the DART rate. Remember: DART only counts cases with days away, restricted duty, or transfers -- not all recordable cases. OSHA uses the DART rate for its Site-Specific Targeting (SST) program.

formulas.name: Severity Rate
formulas.description: Measures the severity of injuries by calculating the number of lost workdays per 100 full-time employees. While the incident rate tells you how often injuries occur, the severity rate tells you how serious they are. A company could have a low incident rate but a high severity rate if injuries are severe.

variables.name: Days Away from Work
variables.description: Total number of calendar days away from work for all cases

variables.name: Employee Hours Worked
variables.description: Total hours worked by all employees during the period

variables.name: Standardization Factor
variables.description: Equivalent of 100 full-time employees working 2,000 hours/year

workedExample.problem: A warehouse had 5 lost-time injuries resulting in a total of 45 lost workdays. Total hours worked were 250,000. Calculate the severity rate.
steps: Step 1: Identify values. Total days lost = 45, EH = 250,000 hours.
Step 2: Apply the formula. SR = (45 x 200,000) / 250,000
workedExample.answer: Severity Rate = 36.0 lost workdays per 100 full-time employees

formulas.examTip: The severity rate uses days lost rather than number of cases. Don't confuse it with TCIR or DART which count cases. Some questions may give you both days lost and number of cases to see if you use the right numerator.

formulas.name: Frequency Rate
formulas.description: An older metric that calculates the number of disabling injuries per one million employee hours worked. While OSHA incidence rates use a 200,000 constant, the traditional frequency rate uses 1,000,000. Less commonly used today but still appears on the CSP exam.

variables.name: Number of Disabling Injuries
variables.description: Number of injuries causing lost time

variables.name: Employee Hours Worked
variables.description: Total hours worked by all employees during the period

variables.name: Standardization Factor
variables.description: Per one million hours worked base

workedExample.problem: A construction company had 6 disabling injuries and 800,000 employee hours worked. Calculate the frequency rate.
steps: Step 1: Identify values. N = 6, EH = 800,000 hours.
workedExample.answer: Frequency Rate = 7.5 disabling injuries per million hours worked

formulas.examTip: Watch for the different multiplier. OSHA rates use 200,000 while the traditional frequency rate uses 1,000,000. The exam may test whether you know which constant to use based on the question wording.

formulas.name: Experience Modification Rate (EMR)
formulas.description: A multiplier applied to workers compensation premiums based on a company's claims history compared to the industry average. An EMR of 1.0 means average performance. Below 1.0 means better than average (lower premiums). Above 1.0 means worse than average (higher premiums). Calculated by the rating bureau (NCCI in most states) using 3 years of data, excluding the most recent year.

variables.name: Experience Modification Rate
variables.description: Multiplier applied to base workers comp premium

variables.name: Actual Incurred Losses
variables.description: The company's actual workers compensation claims costs

variables.name: Expected Losses
variables.description: Average losses for similar companies in the same industry and state

workedExample.problem: A company's base workers compensation premium is $50,000 and their EMR is 0.85. What is their actual premium? What would it be if their EMR were 1.25?
steps: Step 1: Actual Premium = Base Premium x EMR
Step 2: With EMR of 0.85: Premium = $50,000 x 0.85 = $42,500
Step 3: With EMR of 1.25: Premium = $50,000 x 1.25 = $62,500
workedExample.answer: With EMR 0.85, the premium is $42,500 (saving $7,500). With EMR 1.25, the premium is $62,500 (paying $12,500 extra). The EMR difference of 0.40 results in a $20,000 annual premium difference.

formulas.examTip: The CSP exam tests EMR conceptually. Know that EMR is calculated from 3 years of data (excluding the most recent year), an EMR of 1.0 is industry average, and it directly multiplies the workers comp premium. Also know that frequency of claims tends to impact EMR more than severity.

formulas.name: Time-Weighted Average (TWA)
formulas.description: Calculates the average airborne concentration of a substance over an 8-hour work shift, accounting for different exposure levels during different time periods. This is the most fundamental industrial hygiene calculation. The denominator is always 8 hours for a full shift, even if the worker was only exposed for part of the shift (zero concentration is assumed for unexposed time).

variables.name: Concentration
variables.description: Measured airborne concentration during each time period

variables.name: Time
variables.description: Duration of each exposure period

variables.name: Full Shift Duration
variables.description: Standard 8-hour work shift (always 8, even for partial shifts)

workedExample.problem: A worker is exposed to toluene at the following levels: 80 ppm for 3 hours, 110 ppm for 2 hours, 40 ppm for 2 hours, and 0 ppm for 1 hour (lunch break). The TLV is 50 ppm. Calculate the TWA and determine if the exposure is within limits.
steps: Step 1: Multiply each concentration by its time period.
Step 3: Verify total time = 3 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 8 hours (full shift).
Step 5: Compare to TLV. 67.5 ppm > 50 ppm TLV, so the exposure EXCEEDS the limit.
workedExample.answer: TWA = 67.5 ppm, which exceeds the TLV of 50 ppm. The exposure is NOT within acceptable limits.

formulas.examTip: Always divide by 8 hours regardless of actual exposure time. If a worker is only exposed for 6 hours, the other 2 hours are considered 0 concentration. The exam loves giving partial-shift scenarios to test this. Also verify that all time periods add up to 8 hours.

formulas.name: Mixture TLV (Additive Formula)
formulas.description: Evaluates the combined exposure when a worker is exposed to multiple chemicals that have similar toxicological effects (additive effects). Each chemical's concentration is divided by its respective TLV, and the fractions are summed. If the sum exceeds 1.0, the mixture TLV is exceeded. This is based on the assumption that the chemicals act on the same target organ or system.

variables.name: Equivalent Mixture Exposure
variables.description: Sum of exposure fractions; if greater than 1, TLV is exceeded

variables.name: Concentration of Each Chemical
variables.description: Measured airborne concentration of each component

variables.name: Threshold Limit Value
variables.description: Occupational exposure limit for each individual chemical

workedExample.problem: A worker is simultaneously exposed to three solvents: Toluene at 30 ppm (TLV = 50 ppm), Xylene at 60 ppm (TLV = 100 ppm), and MEK at 100 ppm (TLV = 200 ppm). Are they overexposed?
steps: 1.70 > 1.0, so the mixture TLV is EXCEEDED.
Step 4: Note that each chemical individually is below its TLV, but the combined effect exceeds the limit.
workedExample.answer: Em = 1.70, which exceeds 1.0. The worker is overexposed even though no single chemical exceeds its individual TLV.

formulas.examTip: This formula only applies when chemicals have ADDITIVE effects (same target organ). For independent effects, compare each chemical to its own TLV separately. The exam will typically specify that chemicals act additively or state they affect the same organ system.

formulas.name: ppm to mg/m3 Conversion
formulas.description: Converts an airborne concentration from parts per million (a volume/volume ratio) to milligrams per cubic meter (a mass/volume ratio). The factor 24.45 is the molar volume of a gas at standard conditions (25 degrees C and 1 atm). This conversion is essential because OSHA PELs are sometimes given in ppm while TLVs are in mg/m3, or vice versa.

variables.name: Parts Per Million
variables.description: Concentration in parts per million by volume

variables.name: Molecular Weight
variables.description: Molecular weight of the substance

variables.name: Molar Volume
variables.description: Molar volume of ideal gas at 25 degrees C and 1 atm

workedExample.problem: Convert 100 ppm of benzene (MW = 78 g/mol) to mg/m3.
steps: Step 1: Identify values. ppm = 100, MW = 78 g/mol.
workedExample.answer: 100 ppm of benzene = 319.0 mg/m3

formulas.examTip: Memorize 24.45 as the molar volume constant. The exam may give you the molecular weight or expect you to calculate it from the chemical formula. Remember: 24.45 is only valid at standard conditions (25 C, 1 atm). This formula only works for gases and vapors, not particulates.

formulas.name: mg/m3 to ppm Conversion
formulas.description: Converts an airborne concentration from milligrams per cubic meter to parts per million. This is the inverse of the ppm-to-mg/m3 conversion. Use when you have a mass-based measurement and need a volume-based concentration for comparison to standards.

variables.name: Milligrams per Cubic Meter
variables.description: Mass concentration in air

variables.name: Molecular Weight
variables.description: Molecular weight of the substance

variables.name: Molar Volume
variables.description: Molar volume of ideal gas at 25 degrees C and 1 atm

workedExample.problem: A sampling result shows 150 mg/m3 of acetone (MW = 58 g/mol). Convert to ppm and compare to the OSHA PEL of 1000 ppm.
steps: Step 1: Identify values. mg/m3 = 150, MW = 58 g/mol.
Step 5: Compare. 63.2 ppm is well below the 1000 ppm PEL.
workedExample.answer: 150 mg/m3 of acetone = 63.2 ppm, well within the 1000 ppm OSHA PEL

formulas.examTip: Remember the relationship: ppm has 24.45 on top (multiply), mg/m3 has 24.45 on bottom (divide). A helpful mnemonic: "ppm pushes 24.45 up." Always check if the question gives MW or if you need to calculate it.

formulas.name: Air Sampling Concentration
formulas.expression: C = W / V, where V = Q x t
formulas.description: Determines the airborne concentration of a contaminant from a sampling result. The mass of contaminant collected on the sampling media is divided by the total volume of air sampled. Air volume is calculated by multiplying the pump flow rate by the sampling time.

variables.name: Concentration
variables.description: Airborne concentration of the contaminant

variables.name: Weight (Mass) Collected
variables.description: Mass of contaminant collected on the sampling media

variables.name: Volume of Air Sampled
variables.description: Total volume of air pulled through the sampler

variables.name: Flow Rate
variables.description: Sampling pump flow rate

variables.name: Sampling Time
variables.description: Duration of sampling

workedExample.problem: A personal air sample collected 0.5 mg of dust. The pump ran at 2.0 L/min for 480 minutes. Calculate the concentration in mg/m3.
steps: Step 1: Calculate air volume. V = Q x t = 2.0 L/min x 480 min = 960 L
Step 2: Convert liters to cubic meters. 960 L / 1000 = 0.96 m3
Step 3: Calculate concentration. C = W / V = 0.5 mg / 0.96 m3
workedExample.answer: Concentration = 0.52 mg/m3

formulas.examTip: Watch units carefully. Flow rates are usually in L/min and concentrations are in mg/m3, so you need to convert L to m3 (divide by 1000). Also, 480 minutes = 8 hours. The exam may give time in hours, requiring you to convert to minutes first.

formulas.name: Hazard Quotient
formulas.description: A simple ratio comparing a worker's actual exposure to the occupational exposure limit. If HQ is less than 1, the exposure is within acceptable limits. If HQ equals or exceeds 1, the exposure exceeds the limit. This is functionally similar to one term in the mixture formula.

variables.name: Hazard Quotient
variables.description: Ratio of exposure to limit; values >= 1 indicate overexposure

variables.name: Measured Exposure
variables.description: The worker's actual exposure (e.g., TWA)

variables.name: Occupational Exposure Limit
variables.description: The applicable limit (PEL, TLV, REL, etc.)

workedExample.problem: A worker's 8-hour TWA exposure to formaldehyde is 0.6 ppm. The OSHA PEL is 0.75 ppm. Calculate the hazard quotient.
steps: Step 3: Since 0.80 < 1.0, the exposure is within acceptable limits.
Step 4: However, this is above the action level (typically 50% of PEL = 0.375 ppm), so monitoring and medical surveillance may still be required.
workedExample.answer: HQ = 0.80. Exposure is within limits but above the action level.

formulas.examTip: The hazard quotient concept is fundamental. Know that HQ < 1.0 means compliant, HQ >= 1.0 means overexposure. Also know the concept of the action level (typically 50% of PEL, or HQ = 0.5), which triggers monitoring and medical surveillance requirements.

formulas.name: Silica PEL Calculation (Historical)
formulas.description: The historical OSHA formula for calculating the permissible exposure limit for respirable crystalline silica based on the percentage of quartz in the dust. Note: OSHA updated the silica PEL to a fixed 50 micrograms/m3 in 2016 for general industry and construction, but the old formula may still appear on the CSP exam as it tests understanding of how exposure limits relate to silica content.

variables.name: Permissible Exposure Limit
variables.description: Maximum allowable respirable dust concentration

variables.name: Percent Quartz
variables.description: Percentage of crystalline silica (quartz) in the dust sample

variables.name: Numerator Constant
variables.description: Constant for respirable dust formula

variables.name: Denominator Constant
variables.description: Added to percent quartz in denominator

workedExample.problem: Bulk analysis shows a rock dust contains 30% quartz. Calculate the historical OSHA PEL for respirable dust. Compare to the current PEL of 50 micrograms/m3.
steps: Step 4: Compare to current PEL: The current standard of 50 ug/m3 is much more protective than the old formula result of 312.5 ug/m3.
workedExample.answer: Historical PEL = 0.31 mg/m3 (312.5 ug/m3). The current OSHA PEL is 50 ug/m3, which is significantly more restrictive.

formulas.examTip: Know both the historical formula and the current PEL of 50 ug/m3. The exam may test the formula for its mathematical concept. Also know that there was a separate formula for total dust: PEL = 30 / (%SiO2 + 2). The denominator always has "+2".

formulas.name: OSHA Permissible Exposure Time
formulas.description: Calculates the maximum allowable exposure time at a given noise level under OSHA standards. OSHA uses a 5 dB exchange rate (doubling rate), meaning for every 5 dB increase above 90 dBA, the allowable time is cut in half. At 90 dBA, 8 hours is allowed. At 95 dBA, only 4 hours. At 100 dBA, only 2 hours.

variables.name: Permissible Exposure Time
variables.description: Maximum allowed exposure duration at the given noise level

variables.name: Sound Level
variables.description: Measured A-weighted noise level

variables.name: OSHA Criterion Level
variables.description: The level at which 8 hours of exposure is allowed

variables.name: Exchange Rate
variables.description: OSHA uses 5 dB doubling rate (halving rate)

workedExample.problem: A worker is exposed to 97 dBA. What is the maximum allowable exposure time under OSHA?
steps: Step 5: Convert to hours and minutes. 3.03 hours = 3 hours and 2 minutes
workedExample.answer: Maximum allowable exposure at 97 dBA is approximately 3 hours under OSHA standards.

formulas.examTip: Memorize the key OSHA noise values: 90 dBA = 8 hr, 95 dBA = 4 hr, 100 dBA = 2 hr, 105 dBA = 1 hr, 110 dBA = 30 min, 115 dBA = 15 min. The exam expects you to know that OSHA uses 90 dBA criterion level with a 5 dB exchange rate, while ACGIH uses 85 dBA with a 3 dB exchange rate.

formulas.name: ACGIH Allowable Exposure Time
formulas.description: Calculates the maximum allowable exposure time at a given noise level under ACGIH guidelines. ACGIH uses a stricter 3 dB exchange rate and a lower criterion level of 85 dBA. This means for every 3 dB increase above 85 dBA, the allowable time is cut in half. The ACGIH standard is more protective than OSHA.

variables.name: Allowable Exposure Time
variables.description: Maximum allowed exposure duration

variables.name: Sound Level
variables.description: Measured A-weighted noise level

variables.name: ACGIH Criterion Level
variables.description: The level at which 8 hours of exposure is allowed under ACGIH

variables.name: Exchange Rate
variables.description: ACGIH uses 3 dB doubling rate

workedExample.problem: Compare the allowable time at 97 dBA under OSHA vs. ACGIH.
steps: Step 3: Compare. OSHA allows 3 hours; ACGIH allows only 30 minutes.
Step 4: The ACGIH standard is significantly more protective at higher noise levels.
workedExample.answer: At 97 dBA: OSHA allows 3.03 hours, ACGIH allows only 0.5 hours (30 minutes). ACGIH is 6 times more restrictive.

formulas.examTip: The exam will test whether you know the difference between OSHA (90 dBA, 5 dB exchange) and ACGIH (85 dBA, 3 dB exchange). The 3 dB exchange rate is based on equal energy and is considered more scientifically accurate. NIOSH also uses 85 dBA criterion with 3 dB exchange.

formulas.name: Noise Dose
formulas.description: Calculates the total noise dose as a percentage when a worker is exposed to varying noise levels throughout the shift. Each period's actual exposure time (C) is divided by the allowable time (T) at that level. If the sum exceeds 1.0 (or dose exceeds 100%), the exposure limit is exceeded. This is identical in concept to the mixture TLV formula but applied to noise.

variables.name: Noise Dose
variables.description: Total noise dose as a percentage; 100% = maximum allowed

variables.name: Actual Exposure Time
variables.description: Time actually spent at each noise level

variables.name: Allowed Exposure Time
variables.description: Maximum allowed time at that noise level (from T formula)

workedExample.problem: A worker is exposed to 90 dBA for 4 hours, 95 dBA for 2 hours, and 100 dBA for 2 hours. Calculate the OSHA noise dose.
steps: Step 1: Find allowed times using T = 8/2^((L-90)/5).
Step 4: Convert to percentage. D = 2.00 x 100% = 200%
Step 5: Since 200% > 100%, the exposure EXCEEDS the OSHA limit.
workedExample.answer: Noise Dose = 200%. This is twice the allowable dose, indicating significant overexposure.

formulas.examTip: The noise dose formula is structurally identical to the mixture TLV formula. OSHA action level is 50% dose (triggers hearing conservation program). 100% dose = PEL. Make sure you use the correct T formula (OSHA or ACGIH) based on what the question asks.

formulas.name: TWA from Noise Dose
formulas.description: Converts a noise dose percentage to an equivalent 8-hour time-weighted average noise level in dBA. This is the OSHA method for converting dosimeter readings to TWA values. The 16.61 constant and 90 dBA reference are based on the OSHA 5 dB exchange rate.

variables.name: Time-Weighted Average
variables.description: Equivalent continuous noise level for 8 hours

variables.name: Dose
variables.description: Measured noise dose as a percentage

variables.name: OSHA Constant
variables.description: Constant derived from OSHA 5 dB exchange rate (= 5/log2)

variables.name: Criterion Level
variables.description: OSHA criterion level

workedExample.problem: A dosimeter reads a noise dose of 200%. What is the 8-hour TWA?
workedExample.answer: TWA = 95.0 dBA. This makes sense because a 200% dose means the worker received twice the allowable dose, which corresponds to 95 dBA (5 dB above the 90 dBA criterion).

formulas.examTip: The constant 16.61 comes from 5/log(2). For ACGIH with a 3 dB exchange rate, the constant would be 10.0 (3/log2 = 9.97, rounded to 10) and the reference would be 85 dBA. Verify your answer makes sense: 100% dose = 90 dBA TWA, 50% dose = 85 dBA TWA, 200% dose = 95 dBA TWA.

formulas.name: Sound Level Addition (Combining Sources)
formulas.description: Calculates the combined sound level when two or more independent sound sources are present. Sound levels in decibels cannot be added directly because decibels are logarithmic. This formula converts each source to its linear power, adds them, and converts back to decibels. A key rule of thumb: combining two equal sources adds 3 dB.

variables.name: Combined Sound Level
variables.description: Resulting sound level from all sources combined

variables.name: Individual Sound Levels
variables.description: Sound level of each source measured separately

workedExample.problem: A machine produces 88 dBA and another machine nearby produces 92 dBA. What is the combined noise level?
steps: Step 1: Convert each level to linear power.
Alternative shortcut: The difference is 4 dB, so add approximately 1.5 dB to the higher level: 92 + 1.5 = 93.5 dBA.
workedExample.answer: Combined level = 93.5 dBA. Note the combined level is only 1.5 dB above the louder source.

formulas.examTip: Memorize these shortcuts for combining two equal sources: two sources at the same level add 3 dB. Two sources 3 dB apart add about 1.8 dB to the louder one. Two sources 6 dB apart add about 1.0 dB. Two sources 10+ dB apart: the quieter one is negligible.

formulas.name: Distance Attenuation (Inverse Square Law for Sound)
formulas.description: Calculates the change in sound pressure level when the distance from a point source changes. For every doubling of distance from a point source in a free field (no reflections), the sound level drops by 6 dB. This is derived from the inverse square law applied to sound intensity.

variables.name: Sound Level at Distance d1
variables.description: Known sound level at the reference distance

variables.name: Sound Level at Distance d2
variables.description: Predicted sound level at the new distance

variables.name: Reference Distance
variables.description: Distance where sound level is known

variables.name: New Distance
variables.description: Distance where sound level is being predicted

workedExample.problem: A machine measures 100 dBA at 3 feet. What is the sound level at 12 feet?
steps: Step 1: Identify values. L1 = 100 dBA, d1 = 3 ft, d2 = 12 ft.
Verification: 12 feet is 4x the distance (2 doublings), so the level drops by 2 x 6 = 12 dB. 100 - 12 = 88 dBA. This confirms our answer.
workedExample.answer: Sound level at 12 feet = 88 dBA (approximately). The level dropped 12 dB because the distance quadrupled (two doublings, each losing 6 dB).

formulas.examTip: Remember: doubling distance = 6 dB reduction for a point source in free field. This only works for point sources (single machines) in outdoor/free-field conditions. Indoor environments have reflections that reduce the actual attenuation. The exam may also test line sources, which lose only 3 dB per doubling.

formulas.name: NRR to Estimated Attenuation
formulas.description: Converts a hearing protector's Noise Reduction Rating (NRR) to an estimated real-world attenuation in dBA. The NRR is measured on the C-weighted scale, so 7 dB is subtracted to approximate the A-weighted correction, and the result is divided by 2 to account for real-world fit factors (the OSHA derating method). This gives a conservative estimate of actual protection.

variables.name: Noise Reduction Rating
variables.description: Manufacturer's rated noise reduction from EPA label (C-weighted)

variables.name: C-to-A Correction
variables.description: Approximate correction from C-weighted to A-weighted scale

variables.name: Derating Factor
variables.description: Accounts for real-world fitting (50% derating)

workedExample.problem: A worker in 105 dBA noise wears earplugs with NRR 29. Is the protected exposure below the 90 dBA PEL?
steps: Step 3: Compare to PEL. 94 dBA > 90 dBA PEL.
Step 4: The hearing protection alone does NOT reduce exposure below the PEL.
Step 5: Additional controls or dual protection would be needed.
workedExample.answer: Estimated attenuation = 11 dB. Protected exposure = 94 dBA, which still exceeds the 90 dBA PEL.

formulas.examTip: OSHA uses this single derating method: (NRR-7)/2. NIOSH uses different derating factors by protector type: earplugs are derated by 50% (NRR x 0.5), earmuffs by 25% (NRR x 0.75), and dual protection uses the higher NRR x 0.75 plus 5 dB. The exam usually tests the OSHA method.

formulas.name: Sound Power Level
formulas.description: Converts sound power in watts to sound power level in decibels. Sound power is the total acoustic energy emitted by a source per unit time and is independent of distance and environment. The reference power W0 is 10^-12 watts (1 picowatt).

variables.name: Sound Power Level
variables.description: Sound power expressed in decibels

variables.name: Sound Power
variables.description: Acoustic power output of the source

variables.name: Reference Power
variables.description: Reference value = 10^-12 watts (1 picowatt)

workedExample.problem: A machine has an acoustic power output of 0.01 watts. What is its sound power level?
steps: Step 1: Identify values. W = 0.01 watts, W0 = 10^-12 watts.
Step 4: Multiply by 10. Lw = 10 x 10 = 100 dB
workedExample.answer: Sound Power Level = 100 dB

formulas.examTip: Sound power level (Lw) is a property of the source and is constant regardless of the environment. Sound pressure level (Lp) varies with distance and surroundings. Know the difference. The exam may test conceptual understanding rather than calculation.

formulas.name: Sound Pressure Level
formulas.description: Converts sound pressure in Pascals to sound pressure level in decibels. Sound pressure is what is actually measured by sound level meters and is what the ear responds to. The reference pressure P0 is 20 micropascals (20 x 10^-6 Pa), which is the threshold of human hearing at 1000 Hz.

variables.name: Sound Pressure Level
variables.description: Sound pressure expressed in decibels

variables.name: Sound Pressure
variables.description: Measured root-mean-square sound pressure

variables.name: Reference Pressure
variables.description: Reference value = 20 x 10^-6 Pa (20 micropascals)

workedExample.problem: A sound level meter measures a sound pressure of 2 Pa. What is the sound pressure level?
steps: Step 1: Identify values. P = 2 Pa, P0 = 20 x 10^-6 Pa.
Step 4: Multiply by 20. Lp = 20 x 5 = 100 dB
workedExample.answer: Sound Pressure Level = 100 dB

formulas.examTip: Note the multiplier is 20 for pressure (not 10 as for power). This is because power is proportional to pressure squared (P^2), and 10 x log(P^2) = 20 x log(P). The threshold of pain is approximately 140 dB (200 Pa).

formulas.name: WBGT Outdoor (with Solar Load)
formulas.description: Calculates the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature index for outdoor conditions with direct sunlight. WBGT is the primary heat stress index used by OSHA and ACGIH. It weighs humidity most heavily (70% from natural wet bulb), radiant heat (20% from globe thermometer), and air temperature (10% from dry bulb). The dominance of humidity reflects that evaporative cooling is the body's primary heat dissipation mechanism.

variables.name: Natural Wet Bulb Temperature
variables.description: Measures the cooling effect of evaporation (humidity). Uses a naturally ventilated wet wick, NOT a sling psychrometer.

variables.name: Globe Temperature
variables.description: Measured inside a 6-inch matte black copper globe; indicates radiant heat load

variables.name: Dry Bulb Temperature
variables.description: Standard air temperature measured in shade

workedExample.problem: At an outdoor construction site, the natural wet bulb temperature is 78 F, the globe temperature is 110 F, and the dry bulb temperature is 95 F. Calculate the WBGT.
steps: Step 1: Identify values. Tnwb = 78 F, Tg = 110 F, Tdb = 95 F.
Step 4: Compare to ACGIH TLV action limit for moderate work: 82.0 F (continuous work). This WBGT exceeds the limit for continuous moderate work.
workedExample.answer: WBGT = 86.1 F. Work-rest cycles or other controls would be needed for moderate or heavy work.

formulas.examTip: Memorize the coefficients: 0.7 (NWB), 0.2 (Globe), 0.1 (Dry Bulb) for outdoor. The natural wet bulb dominates because humidity is the most critical factor. WBGT uses natural wet bulb (not psychrometric wet bulb). Know that metabolic heat must also be considered when comparing to TLVs.

formulas.name: WBGT Indoor (No Solar Load)
formulas.description: Calculates the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature index for indoor conditions or outdoor conditions without direct sunlight. The dry bulb temperature is dropped because without solar radiation, the globe temperature adequately accounts for both radiant heat and air temperature. The globe temperature coefficient increases from 0.2 to 0.3 to compensate.

variables.name: Natural Wet Bulb Temperature
variables.description: Measures evaporative cooling potential (humidity effect)

variables.name: Globe Temperature
variables.description: Radiant heat measured by black globe thermometer

workedExample.problem: Inside a steel mill, the natural wet bulb temperature is 82 F and the globe temperature is 130 F. Calculate the WBGT.
steps: Step 1: Identify values. Tnwb = 82 F, Tg = 130 F. (No direct sun, so use indoor formula.)
Step 4: This extremely high WBGT indicates severe heat stress conditions.
workedExample.answer: WBGT = 96.4 F. This exceeds TLVs for all but the lightest work, even with work-rest cycles.

formulas.examTip: Indoor formula has only TWO terms (no dry bulb). The exam will tell you whether it is indoor or outdoor. If there is no direct sunlight, even outdoors, use the indoor formula. Coefficient on globe increases from 0.2 to 0.3 to absorb the missing dry bulb contribution.

formulas.name: Airflow (Q = AV)
formulas.description: The fundamental ventilation equation relating airflow volume to duct cross-sectional area and air velocity. This is the most basic and commonly used ventilation formula. It states that the volume of air flowing through a duct equals the cross-sectional area of the duct multiplied by the velocity of the air.

variables.name: Airflow (Volume Flow Rate)
variables.description: Volume of air passing through a point per unit time

variables.name: Cross-Sectional Area
variables.description: Area of the duct or opening perpendicular to airflow

variables.name: Velocity
variables.description: Speed of the air moving through the duct

workedExample.problem: A round duct has a diameter of 12 inches. Air velocity is measured at 3,500 fpm. Calculate the airflow in cfm.
steps: Step 1: Convert diameter to feet. 12 inches / 12 = 1.0 ft. Radius = 0.5 ft.
Step 2: Calculate area. A = pi x r^2 = 3.14159 x (0.5)^2 = 0.7854 ft2
Step 3: Apply formula. Q = A x V = 0.7854 x 3,500 = 2,749 cfm
workedExample.answer: Airflow = 2,749 cfm

formulas.examTip: Always convert inches to feet before calculating area. A 12-inch duct has an area of 0.7854 ft2, not 113 in2 (unless you convert cfm units too). This formula is rearranged frequently: V = Q/A to find velocity, or A = Q/V to size a duct.

formulas.name: Velocity from Velocity Pressure
formulas.description: Calculates air velocity from a velocity pressure measurement taken with a pitot tube and manometer. The constant 4005 applies to standard air (70 F, 29.92 in Hg, density 0.075 lb/ft3). Velocity pressure is always positive and represents the kinetic energy of the air.

variables.name: Air Velocity
variables.description: Speed of airflow in feet per minute

variables.name: Velocity Pressure
variables.description: Pressure due to air motion, measured with a pitot tube

variables.name: Constant
variables.description: Valid for standard air density (0.075 lb/ft3)

workedExample.problem: A pitot tube traverse of a duct gives an average velocity pressure of 0.75 inches w.g. Calculate the duct velocity.
workedExample.answer: Air velocity = 3,468 fpm

formulas.examTip: The constant 4005 is specific to standard air. At non-standard conditions, use 4005 x sqrt(df/da) where df is standard density and da is actual density. A pitot tube measures total pressure (TP) minus static pressure (SP) to get VP. VP is always positive.

formulas.name: Hood Static Pressure Entry Loss
formulas.description: Calculates the velocity of air entering a hood from the hood static pressure and the coefficient of entry. The coefficient of entry (Ce) accounts for energy losses as air enters the hood. A plain duct opening has Ce = 0.72, a flanged opening has Ce = 0.82, and a bell-mouth entry has Ce = 0.98.

variables.name: Entry Velocity
variables.description: Speed of air entering the hood

variables.name: Coefficient of Entry
variables.description: Efficiency factor for the hood design (0 to 1.0)

variables.name: Hood Static Pressure
variables.description: Static pressure measured just downstream of the hood (absolute value)

variables.name: Standard Air Constant
variables.description: Same constant as velocity pressure equation

workedExample.problem: A flanged hood (Ce = 0.82) has a static pressure of -2.0 in. w.g. Calculate the air velocity entering the hood.
steps: Step 1: Use absolute value of SPh = 2.0 in. w.g.
workedExample.answer: Hood entry velocity = 4,643 fpm

formulas.examTip: Know the common Ce values: plain opening = 0.72, flanged = 0.82, bell-mouth = 0.98. Higher Ce means more efficient (less entry loss). Adding a flange is one of the simplest improvements to a hood. Hood static pressure is always measured as negative (suction) but use the absolute value in calculations.

formulas.name: Total Pressure Relationship
formulas.description: The fundamental relationship between total pressure, static pressure, and velocity pressure in a duct system. Total pressure represents the total energy of the air. Static pressure is the potential energy (can be positive or negative), and velocity pressure is the kinetic energy (always positive). This is based on the conservation of energy principle (Bernoulli).

variables.name: Total Pressure
variables.description: Total energy of the air stream (always decreases in direction of flow due to friction)

variables.name: Static Pressure
variables.description: Pressure acting equally in all directions; can be positive or negative

variables.name: Velocity Pressure
variables.description: Pressure due to air motion; always positive

workedExample.problem: At a point in a duct, the static pressure is -3.5 in. w.g. and the velocity pressure is 1.2 in. w.g. Calculate the total pressure.
steps: Step 3: The negative TP indicates this point is on the suction side of the fan.
workedExample.answer: Total Pressure = -2.3 in. w.g.

formulas.examTip: Remember: VP is always positive, SP can be positive or negative, and TP always decreases in the direction of airflow (due to friction losses). On the suction side of the fan, SP is negative. On the discharge side, SP is positive. TP changes across the fan (fan adds energy).

formulas.name: Fan Static Pressure
formulas.description: Calculates the fan static pressure, which represents the useful static pressure generated by the fan. It equals the difference between the static pressure at the fan outlet and inlet, minus the velocity pressure at the inlet (which represents kinetic energy the fan must overcome). FSP is used for fan selection and system design.

variables.name: Fan Static Pressure
variables.description: Useful static pressure produced by the fan

variables.name: Static Pressure at Fan Outlet
variables.description: Static pressure on the discharge side of the fan

variables.name: Static Pressure at Fan Inlet
variables.description: Static pressure on the suction side of the fan (usually negative)

variables.name: Velocity Pressure at Fan Inlet
variables.description: Velocity pressure entering the fan

workedExample.problem: A fan has inlet static pressure of -4.0 in. w.g., outlet static pressure of +2.5 in. w.g., and inlet velocity pressure of 0.8 in. w.g. Calculate the fan static pressure.
workedExample.answer: Fan Static Pressure = 5.7 in. w.g.

formulas.examTip: Be very careful with the signs. SPin is usually negative (suction), so subtracting a negative makes it positive. The exam loves to test sign errors in this formula. FSP is always positive for a properly operating fan.

formulas.name: Capture Velocity (Plain Round Opening)
formulas.description: Calculates the capture velocity at a given distance from a plain (unflanged) round hood opening. The velocity decreases rapidly with distance from the hood face. At the hood face (x = 0), velocity equals Q/A. At one duct diameter away, velocity drops significantly. This formula shows why hoods must be positioned as close to the source as possible.

variables.name: Capture Velocity
variables.description: Air velocity at distance x from the hood face

variables.name: Airflow Volume
variables.description: Volume of air being exhausted through the hood

variables.name: Distance from Hood Face
variables.description: Distance from the face of the hood opening to the capture point

variables.name: Area of Hood Opening
variables.description: Cross-sectional area of the hood opening

workedExample.problem: A 6-inch diameter plain round hood exhausts 500 cfm. What is the capture velocity at 12 inches (1 ft) from the hood face?
steps: Step 1: Calculate area. Diameter = 6 in = 0.5 ft. A = pi x (0.25)^2 = 0.1963 ft2
Step 5: Compare to face velocity: at x = 0, V = 500/0.1963 = 2,548 fpm.
Step 6: Velocity dropped from 2,548 fpm to 49 fpm at just 1 ft away -- a 98% reduction.
workedExample.answer: Capture velocity at 1 ft = 49 fpm. This demonstrates why hoods must be placed very close to the source.

formulas.examTip: The key insight: capture velocity drops dramatically with distance. At one duct diameter away, velocity is about 10% of face velocity. A flanged hood improves capture by about 25% at the same distance. Remember that recommended capture velocities range from 50 fpm (low toxicity, still air) to 500 fpm (high toxicity, active generation).

formulas.name: Dilution Ventilation
formulas.description: Calculates the volume of air needed to dilute a contaminant to an acceptable concentration. This is the simplest form of general dilution ventilation. In practice, a safety factor (K) is often applied: Q = (G x K) / C. Dilution ventilation is suitable for low-toxicity vapors, uniform generation, and when the source cannot be enclosed. It is NOT appropriate for highly toxic substances or particulates.

variables.name: Required Airflow
variables.description: Volume of dilution air needed

variables.name: Generation Rate
variables.description: Rate at which contaminant vapor is generated

variables.name: Target Concentration
variables.description: Desired maximum concentration

variables.name: Safety Factor (Mixing Factor)
variables.description: Accounts for imperfect mixing (typically 3-10)

workedExample.problem: A solvent evaporates at a rate of 0.5 cfm of vapor. The TLV is 100 ppm and a safety factor of 5 is used. How much dilution air is needed?
steps: Step 1: Convert TLV to fraction. 100 ppm = 100/1,000,000 = 0.0001
Step 2: Apply formula with safety factor. Q = (G x K) / C
workedExample.answer: Required dilution airflow = 25,000 cfm. This large volume illustrates why local exhaust ventilation is generally preferred over dilution ventilation.

formulas.examTip: Dilution ventilation requires large air volumes and is only appropriate for low toxicity, low generation rate situations. The exam may test when dilution is appropriate vs. local exhaust. Dilution is NOT recommended for: highly toxic substances, dust/particulates, variable generation rates, or when workers are between the source and exhaust.

formulas.name: Inverse Square Law (Radiation)
formulas.description: Calculates how radiation intensity changes with distance from a point source. As distance doubles, intensity drops to one-quarter. This is one of the three cardinal principles of radiation protection (time, distance, shielding). Distance is the most practical and effective protection method.

variables.name: Intensity at Distance d1
variables.description: Known radiation intensity at the reference distance

variables.name: Intensity at Distance d2
variables.description: Predicted intensity at the new distance

variables.name: Reference Distance
variables.description: Distance where intensity is known

variables.name: New Distance
variables.description: Distance where intensity is being predicted

workedExample.problem: A radiation source measures 200 mR/hr at 2 feet. What is the intensity at 8 feet? What distance gives 5 mR/hr?
steps: Step 3: Rearrange: d2 = d1 x sqrt(I1/I2)
Step 4: d2 = 2 x sqrt(200/5) = 2 x sqrt(40) = 2 x 6.32 = 12.6 feet
workedExample.answer: At 8 feet: I2 = 12.5 mR/hr (16x less at 4x the distance). For 5 mR/hr, stand 12.6 feet away.

formulas.examTip: This formula applies to ALL point sources of radiation, light, and sound. The key concept: doubling distance reduces intensity by 75% (to 1/4). Tripling distance reduces to 1/9. The exam frequently tests this formula. Remember to square the distance ratio, not just the distance.

formulas.name: Gamma Exposure Rate (6CiEf)
formulas.expression: X = 6 x Ci x Ef / d^2
formulas.description: Estimates the gamma radiation exposure rate from a point source. The factor 6 is an approximation that simplifies the specific gamma ray constant for many common isotopes. Ci is the source activity in curies, Ef is the gamma energy factor, and d is distance in feet. For Cobalt-60, Ef is approximately 1.3 R-ft2/Ci-hr.

variables.name: Exposure Rate
variables.description: Gamma radiation exposure rate

variables.name: Approximation Constant
variables.description: Simplified constant for estimation

variables.name: Activity
variables.description: Radioactivity of the source

variables.name: Energy Factor
variables.description: Average gamma energy per disintegration

variables.name: Distance
variables.description: Distance from the source

workedExample.problem: A 10 Ci Cobalt-60 source has a gamma energy factor of 1.3 MeV. What is the exposure rate at 6 feet?
steps: Step 1: Apply formula. X = 6 x Ci x Ef / d^2
workedExample.answer: Exposure rate = 2.17 R/hr at 6 feet. This is a very high exposure rate; the annual occupational dose limit of 5 rem would be reached in about 2.3 hours.

formulas.examTip: The "6CiEf" rule is a quick estimation method. For more precise calculations, use the specific gamma ray constant for the isotope. Know that 1 Curie = 3.7 x 10^10 disintegrations per second. The exam may test this as a quick estimation tool.

formulas.name: Radioactive Decay (Half-Life)
formulas.description: Calculates the remaining activity of a radioactive source after a given time period. Each half-life reduces the activity by half. After 1 half-life, 50% remains. After 2 half-lives, 25% remains. After 7 half-lives, less than 1% remains. After 10 half-lives, the source is generally considered decayed.

variables.name: Remaining Activity
variables.description: Activity remaining after time t

variables.name: Initial Activity
variables.description: Activity at the start (time = 0)

variables.name: Elapsed Time
variables.description: Time that has passed since initial measurement

variables.name: Half-Life
variables.description: Time for activity to decrease by half

workedExample.problem: A 100 mCi Iodine-131 source has a half-life of 8 days. What is the activity after 24 days?
steps: Step 1: Calculate number of half-lives. n = t/t_half = 24/8 = 3 half-lives
workedExample.answer: Activity after 24 days = 12.5 mCi (12.5% of original). Three half-lives have elapsed.

formulas.examTip: The exam often gives non-integer numbers of half-lives. Know how to use fractional exponents: (1/2)^1.5 = 0.354. Also know these benchmarks: 1 half-life = 50%, 2 = 25%, 3 = 12.5%, 4 = 6.25%, 7 = 0.78%, 10 = 0.1%. Know common half-lives: I-131 = 8 days, Co-60 = 5.27 years, Cs-137 = 30 years.

formulas.name: NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)
formulas.expression: RWL = 51 x HM x VM x DM x AM x FM x CM
formulas.description: Calculates the maximum recommended weight for a lifting task under specific conditions. The NIOSH Lifting Equation starts with a load constant of 51 pounds (the maximum weight recommended under ideal conditions) and applies six multipliers that reduce the limit based on actual lifting conditions. Each multiplier ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 represents ideal conditions.

variables.name: Load Constant (LC)
variables.description: Maximum weight under ideal lifting conditions

variables.name: Horizontal Multiplier
variables.description: HM = 10/H, where H = horizontal distance from midpoint of hands to midpoint of ankles (inches). Ideal H = 10 in.

variables.name: Vertical Multiplier
variables.description: VM = 1 - 0.0075|V - 30|, where V = vertical height of hands from floor (inches). Ideal V = 30 in (knuckle height).

variables.name: Distance Multiplier
variables.description: DM = 0.82 + 1.8/D, where D = vertical travel distance of lift (inches). Ideal D = 10 in minimum.

variables.name: Asymmetry Multiplier
variables.description: AM = 1 - 0.0032A, where A = angle of asymmetry (degrees of twisting). Ideal A = 0 degrees.

variables.name: Frequency Multiplier
variables.description: Based on lifting frequency (lifts/min), duration, and vertical region. Obtained from NIOSH tables.

variables.name: Coupling Multiplier
variables.description: Based on quality of hand-to-object coupling: Good = 1.0, Fair = 0.95, Poor = 0.90.

workedExample.problem: A worker lifts boxes from a pallet (H=15 in, V=10 in) to a shelf (V=50 in). Twisting angle is 30 degrees, frequency is 2 lifts/min for 1 hour, and coupling is fair. Calculate the RWL.
steps: FM = 0.84 (from NIOSH table: 2 lifts/min, 1 hour, V < 30 in)
workedExample.answer: RWL = 17.9 lbs. Any load heavier than 17.9 lbs exceeds the recommended limit for this task.

formulas.examTip: Memorize the multiplier formulas: HM = 10/H, VM = 1-0.0075|V-30|, DM = 0.82+1.8/D, AM = 1-0.0032A. Know that ideal conditions give all multipliers = 1.0 (H=10, V=30, D=10, A=0). The RWL is calculated separately at the origin and destination of the lift; the lower value controls.

formulas.name: Lifting Index (LI)
formulas.description: Compares the actual weight being lifted to the recommended weight limit. An LI of 1.0 or less indicates the task is acceptable for most workers. An LI greater than 1.0 indicates increased risk of low back injury. The higher the LI, the greater the risk. NIOSH recommends that tasks with LI > 1.0 be redesigned.

variables.name: Lifting Index
variables.description: Risk indicator; values > 1.0 indicate increased injury risk

variables.name: Actual Weight Lifted
variables.description: The weight of the object being lifted

variables.name: Recommended Weight Limit
variables.description: Maximum recommended weight for the task conditions

workedExample.problem: Using the previous example where RWL = 17.9 lbs, the worker is lifting 35-lb boxes. Calculate the Lifting Index.
steps: Step 3: Since LI = 1.96 > 1.0, the task exceeds the recommended limit.
Step 4: The task should be redesigned. Options include:
- Move the load closer (reduce H to improve HM)
- Raise the origin (adjust V toward 30 inches)
workedExample.answer: LI = 1.96. The task poses a significant risk of back injury and should be redesigned to reduce the LI to 1.0 or below.

formulas.examTip: LI less than or equal to 1.0 = acceptable. LI between 1.0 and 3.0 = some workers at risk. LI greater than 3.0 = many workers at risk. The exam may ask which task modification would most effectively reduce the LI. Reducing H (horizontal distance) usually has the greatest impact.

formulas.name: Ohm's Law
formulas.description: The fundamental relationship between voltage, current, and resistance. Voltage (electromotive force) equals the product of current and resistance. This law governs the behavior of electrical circuits and is essential for understanding electrical hazards. Higher voltage or lower resistance means more current flows through the body.

variables.name: Voltage (EMF)
variables.description: Electrical potential difference driving the current

variables.name: Current
variables.description: Rate of electrical charge flow

variables.name: Resistance
variables.description: Opposition to current flow

workedExample.problem: A worker contacts a 480V circuit. If body resistance is 1,000 ohms (wet skin), what current flows through the worker? Is this lethal?
steps: Step 1: Rearrange for current. I = V/R
480 mA is well above the lethal threshold.
Step 4: Note: dry skin resistance is 100,000+ ohms. Wet skin: 1,000 ohms. Internal body: 300-500 ohms.
workedExample.answer: Current = 480 mA. This far exceeds the 75-100 mA fibrillation threshold and is immediately life-threatening.

formulas.examTip: Current kills, not voltage. Know the physiological thresholds: 1 mA perception, 10-20 mA let-go, 75-100 mA fibrillation, 4+ A cardiac arrest. Skin resistance varies dramatically: dry = 100,000+ ohms, wet = 1,000 ohms, internal = 300-500 ohms. GFCI trips at 5 mA to protect against fibrillation.

formulas.name: Electrical Power
formulas.expression: P = V x I  (also P = I^2 x R  and  P = V^2 / R)
formulas.description: Calculates electrical power, the rate at which electrical energy is converted to other forms (heat, light, motion). Power equals voltage times current. The alternate forms (I^2R and V^2/R) are derived by substituting Ohm's Law and are useful when only two quantities are known.

variables.name: Power
variables.description: Rate of energy conversion or consumption

variables.name: Voltage
variables.description: Electrical potential difference

variables.name: Current
variables.description: Rate of charge flow

variables.name: Resistance
variables.description: Opposition to current flow

workedExample.problem: A 120V circuit has a 15A breaker. What is the maximum power available? If a motor draws 10A, what power does it consume?
steps: Step 1: Maximum power. P = V x I = 120 x 15 = 1,800 W
Step 2: Motor power. P = 120 x 10 = 1,200 W = 1.2 kW
Step 3: Remaining capacity. 1,800 - 1,200 = 600 W available for other loads.
Step 4: Note: 1 horsepower = 746 watts, so 1,200 W is about 1.6 HP.
workedExample.answer: Maximum circuit power = 1,800 W. Motor draws 1,200 W, leaving 600 W capacity.

formulas.examTip: Know all three forms of the power equation. P = I^2R is useful for calculating heat generated in a conductor (which relates to fire hazards from overloaded circuits). Know that 1 HP = 746 W. Circuit breakers protect against overcurrent, which causes overheating (P = I^2R).

formulas.name: Series Resistance
formulas.description: In a series circuit, the total resistance equals the sum of all individual resistances. Current is the same through all components, but voltage divides across them proportionally. Adding resistance in series increases total resistance and decreases current.

variables.name: Total Resistance
variables.description: Combined resistance of all series components

variables.name: Individual Resistances
variables.description: Resistance of each component in the series chain

workedExample.problem: A worker contacts a 240V source. Current flows through: dry skin (100,000 ohms), body (500 ohms), gloves (50,000 ohms), and boot resistance (25,000 ohms). What current flows?
steps: Step 3: This is just above the perception threshold but well below dangerous levels.
Step 4: Without gloves and boots: R = 100,000 + 500 = 100,500 ohms. I = 240/100,500 = 2.4 mA.
Step 5: PPE adds significant resistance in the series path, reducing current.
workedExample.answer: With PPE: I = 1.37 mA (perceivable but safe). Without PPE: I = 2.4 mA. Wet conditions would dramatically increase current by reducing skin resistance.

formulas.examTip: In a series circuit, current is the same everywhere, voltage drops across each resistance. The body, clothing, shoes, gloves, and floor all act as series resistances. This is why insulating PPE is so important -- it adds resistance in series, reducing current.

formulas.name: Parallel Resistance
formulas.description: In a parallel circuit, the reciprocal of total resistance equals the sum of reciprocals of individual resistances. Voltage is the same across all branches, but current divides. The total resistance is always less than the smallest individual resistance. For two resistors: R_total = (R1 x R2)/(R1 + R2).

variables.name: Total Resistance
variables.description: Combined resistance (always less than smallest individual)

variables.name: Individual Resistances
variables.description: Resistance of each parallel branch

workedExample.problem: Two grounding paths exist in parallel: Path A with 10 ohms and Path B with 15 ohms. What is the total ground resistance?
steps: Step 1: Apply parallel formula for two resistors.
Step 3: Note: 6 ohms is less than either individual path (10 or 15 ohms).
Step 4: Adding parallel grounding paths always reduces total ground resistance, improving fault clearing.
workedExample.answer: Total ground resistance = 6 ohms. Multiple ground paths in parallel provide lower resistance and better ground fault protection.

formulas.examTip: Parallel resistance is always less than the smallest individual resistance. This concept applies to grounding systems, where multiple ground paths improve safety. For equal resistors in parallel: R_total = R/n (e.g., three 30-ohm paths in parallel = 10 ohms).

formulas.name: Kinetic Energy
formulas.description: The energy of a moving object, proportional to its mass and the square of its velocity. Since energy is proportional to velocity squared, doubling speed quadruples the energy. This is critical for understanding vehicle safety, machinery guarding, and impact protection.

variables.name: Kinetic Energy
variables.description: Energy due to motion

variables.name: Mass
variables.description: Mass of the moving object

variables.name: Velocity
variables.description: Speed of the object

workedExample.problem: A 5 kg tool falls from a height and hits a worker at 10 m/s. What is the kinetic energy at impact?
steps: Step 1: KE = (1/2) x m x v^2
Step 4: If the tool had been falling at 20 m/s (twice the speed), KE = 0.5 x 5 x 400 = 1,000 J (4x the energy).
workedExample.answer: KE = 250 Joules at impact. Doubling the velocity would quadruple the impact energy to 1,000 J.

formulas.examTip: The v-squared relationship is critical: doubling speed quadruples energy and stopping distance. This applies to forklifts, vehicles, falling objects, and machinery. The exam may test the concept that kinetic energy equals work needed to stop the object (Work = Force x Distance).

formulas.name: Potential Energy (Gravitational)
formulas.expression: PE = m x g x h
formulas.description: The stored energy of an object due to its height above a reference point. When an object falls, potential energy converts to kinetic energy. This is fundamental to fall protection -- a worker at height has potential energy that determines the impact force in a fall.

variables.name: Potential Energy
variables.description: Energy stored due to height/position

variables.name: Mass
variables.description: Mass of the object

variables.name: Gravitational Acceleration
variables.description: Acceleration due to gravity

variables.name: Height
variables.description: Height above the reference point

workedExample.problem: A 90 kg (200 lb) worker is on a platform 10 meters (33 ft) high. What is the potential energy? If the worker falls, what is the velocity at impact?
steps: Step 1: PE = mgh = 90 x 9.81 x 10 = 8,829 J
Step 2: At impact, PE converts to KE. KE = (1/2)mv^2 = PE
Step 3: v = sqrt(2 x PE/m) = sqrt(2 x 8829/90) = sqrt(196.2) = 14.0 m/s
Step 4: 14.0 m/s = 31.3 mph. This explains why falls from heights are often fatal.
workedExample.answer: PE = 8,829 J. Impact velocity = 14.0 m/s (31.3 mph). This demonstrates why fall protection is critical for heights above 6 feet in construction and 4 feet in general industry.

formulas.examTip: The conservation of energy (PE converts to KE) explains why fall distance determines severity. This is related to fall protection concepts: free fall distance, deceleration distance, and maximum arresting force. The exam connects physics to real-world safety applications.

formulas.name: Newton's Second Law (Force)
formulas.description: Force equals mass times acceleration. This is the foundation for understanding impact forces, deceleration injuries, stopping distances, and many mechanical safety calculations. Greater mass or greater deceleration means greater force. In safety, we often want to minimize deceleration (extend stopping distance) to reduce impact forces.

variables.name: Force
variables.description: The force applied to or by the object

variables.name: Mass
variables.description: Mass of the object

variables.name: Acceleration
variables.description: Rate of change of velocity (or deceleration)

workedExample.problem: A 100 kg worker falls and is decelerated by a shock-absorbing lanyard over 1 meter. The fall velocity was 6 m/s. What is the average stopping force?
steps: Step 1: Find deceleration using v^2 = 2 x a x d. a = v^2/(2d) = 36/(2 x 1) = 18 m/s^2
Step 2: Calculate force. F = m x a = 100 x 18 = 1,800 N
Step 3: Convert to pounds. 1,800 N / 4.448 = 405 lbs
Step 4: OSHA limits maximum arresting force (MAF) to 1,800 lbs (8 kN) for body harness systems.
Step 5: Our result of 405 lbs is within limits because the shock absorber extended the stopping distance.
workedExample.answer: Average stopping force = 1,800 N (405 lbs). The shock absorber reduced the force by extending the deceleration distance. Without it, the force would be much higher.

formulas.examTip: F = ma connects to many safety topics: vehicle accidents, falling objects, machine guarding, fall protection. The key concept is that extending deceleration distance (crumple zones, shock absorbers, landing pads) reduces force. OSHA MAF limit is 1,800 lbs for full body harness, 900 lbs for body belt.

formulas.name: Weight
formulas.description: Weight is the force of gravity acting on an object's mass. It is a special case of F = ma where a = g (gravitational acceleration). Weight is measured in Newtons (SI) or pounds (US). Mass and weight are different: mass is constant regardless of location, but weight changes with gravitational field strength.

variables.name: Weight
variables.description: Force due to gravity

variables.name: Mass
variables.description: Amount of matter in the object

variables.name: Gravitational Acceleration
variables.description: Acceleration due to gravity on Earth's surface

workedExample.problem: A 200 kg barrel is stored on a shelf 3 meters high. What is its weight and what would the impact force be if it fell?
steps: Step 1: Weight = mg = 200 x 9.81 = 1,962 N = 441 lbs
Step 2: If it falls 3 m, velocity at impact: v = sqrt(2gh) = sqrt(2 x 9.81 x 3) = 7.67 m/s
Step 3: Impact force depends on stopping distance. If it stops in 0.01 m (hard floor):
workedExample.answer: Weight = 1,962 N (441 lbs). Impact force on a hard floor could exceed 132,000 lbs due to the very short stopping distance.

formulas.examTip: Know the difference between mass and weight. In the US system, we often use pounds for both weight (force) and mass, which can be confusing. In SI, mass is in kg and weight is in Newtons. The exam may test crane/hoist capacity in terms of weight.

formulas.name: Pressure
formulas.description: Pressure is force per unit area. This fundamental relationship applies to many safety scenarios: pressure vessels, hydraulic systems, PPE design, and confined spaces. Higher pressure means greater force on a given area, which relates to rupture risk, penetration hazards, and structural failure.

variables.name: Pressure
variables.description: Force distributed over an area

variables.name: Force
variables.description: Total force applied

variables.name: Area
variables.description: Area over which force is distributed

workedExample.problem: A pressurized vessel has an internal pressure of 150 psi. A circular inspection port has a 6-inch diameter. What is the total force on the port?
steps: Step 1: Rearrange for force. F = P x A
Step 2: Calculate area. A = pi x r^2 = pi x 3^2 = 28.27 in^2
Step 3: F = 150 psi x 28.27 in^2 = 4,241 lbs
Step 4: This is over 2 tons of force on a 6-inch port, illustrating the danger of pressurized systems.
workedExample.answer: Force on the port = 4,241 lbs (approximately 2.1 tons). This demonstrates why pressure vessel integrity and lockout/tagout procedures are critical.

formulas.examTip: Pressure vessel hazards are a significant CSP topic. Know the relationship between pressure, force, and area. Common conversions: 1 atm = 14.7 psi = 101.3 kPa = 760 mmHg. The larger the area, the greater the total force at a given pressure.

formulas.name: Friction Force
formulas.description: The friction force equals the coefficient of friction multiplied by the normal (perpendicular) force. Friction is essential for walking, vehicle braking, and preventing slips. Low friction (slippery surfaces) is a major hazard. The coefficient of friction depends on the materials in contact and whether they are static or kinetic (sliding).

variables.name: Friction Force
variables.description: Force resisting motion between surfaces

variables.name: Coefficient of Friction
variables.description: Ratio of friction force to normal force; depends on materials and conditions

variables.name: Normal Force
variables.description: Force perpendicular to the contact surface (usually weight on flat surfaces)

workedExample.problem: A 180-lb worker walks on a floor with a coefficient of friction of 0.3 (wet tile). Is this adequate? What friction coefficient is needed for safe walking?
steps: Step 1: Friction force available. F_f = 0.3 x 180 = 54 lbs
Step 2: During walking, horizontal forces reach about 20% of body weight.
Step 3: Available (54 lbs) > Required (36 lbs), so the floor provides enough friction under normal walking.
Step 4: However, a coefficient of 0.5 or higher is generally recommended for safe walking. 0.3 may be inadequate for hurried walking or carrying loads.
workedExample.answer: At mu = 0.3, friction is marginally adequate for normal walking but below the 0.5 recommended value. Wet floors should be addressed with slip-resistant treatments, drainage, or footwear.

formulas.examTip: Safe walking requires a coefficient of friction of at least 0.5. Wet surfaces can reduce it to 0.1-0.3. The exam tests slip/fall prevention concepts. Know that static friction (starting movement) is higher than kinetic friction (during sliding). Ramp safety relates to the tangent of the angle vs. the coefficient of friction.

formulas.name: Velocity
formulas.description: Calculates the final velocity of an object given its initial velocity, acceleration, and time. For falling objects, acceleration is gravity (g = 9.81 m/s^2 or 32.2 ft/s^2) and initial velocity is often zero (dropped, not thrown).

variables.name: Final Velocity
variables.description: Speed at the end of the time period

variables.name: Initial Velocity
variables.description: Speed at the start (0 if starting from rest)

variables.name: Acceleration
variables.description: Rate of velocity change (gravity = 9.81 m/s^2)

variables.name: Time
variables.description: Duration of acceleration

workedExample.problem: A tool is dropped from a scaffold. How fast is it traveling after 2 seconds?
steps: Step 1: v0 = 0 (dropped from rest), a = 32.2 ft/s^2, t = 2 s
Step 3: Convert: 64.4 ft/s x (1 mph / 1.467 ft/s) = 43.9 mph
Step 4: Distance fallen: s = v0t + (1/2)at^2 = 0 + 0.5 x 32.2 x 4 = 64.4 ft
workedExample.answer: After 2 seconds: velocity = 64.4 ft/s (43.9 mph), distance fallen = 64.4 ft. This shows the extreme danger of dropped objects even from moderate heights.

formulas.examTip: For falling objects, v0 = 0 and a = g. This formula combined with distance and energy formulas helps calculate impact forces. The exam connects these physics concepts to practical safety scenarios like toe boards, tool lanyards, and barricading below overhead work.

formulas.name: Distance (Displacement)
formulas.expression: s = v0 x t + (1/2) x a x t^2
formulas.description: Calculates the distance traveled by an object under constant acceleration. For objects dropped from rest, v0 = 0 and the formula simplifies to s = (1/2)gt^2. Also used for stopping distance calculations.

variables.name: Distance
variables.description: Total distance traveled

variables.name: Initial Velocity
variables.description: Speed at the start

variables.name: Acceleration
variables.description: Rate of velocity change

variables.name: Time
variables.description: Duration of travel

workedExample.problem: A forklift traveling at 10 mph (14.67 ft/s) applies brakes with a deceleration of 15 ft/s^2. What is the stopping distance?
steps: Step 1: At stop, v = 0. Use v^2 = v0^2 + 2as. Rearrange: s = v0^2 / (2a)
Step 4: Doubling speed quadruples stopping distance (7.17 x 4 = 28.7 ft).
workedExample.answer: Stopping distance at 10 mph = 7.2 ft. At 20 mph = 28.7 ft. Doubling speed quadruples stopping distance.

formulas.examTip: The stopping distance is proportional to v^2, so doubling speed quadruples stopping distance. This concept applies to forklifts, vehicles, and any moving equipment. The exam often tests the v-squared relationship conceptually rather than requiring exact calculations.

formulas.name: Lever / Moment (Torque)
formulas.expression: F1 x d1 = F2 x d2
formulas.description: The principle of moments states that for a lever in equilibrium, the clockwise moment equals the counterclockwise moment. Moment (torque) equals force times the perpendicular distance from the pivot point. This applies to crane stability, scaffold loading, and mechanical advantage calculations.

variables.name: Force 1 (Effort)
variables.description: Applied force on one side of the lever

variables.name: Distance 1 (Effort Arm)
variables.description: Distance from force 1 to the fulcrum

variables.name: Force 2 (Load)
variables.description: Load or resistance on the other side

variables.name: Distance 2 (Load Arm)
